reviews.summary: The paper under reviews studies the problem of optimal linear interpolation. More precisely the authors derive an explicit formula for the *best* linear interpolator that can be written as a linear function of the outputs and as a function of desired quantities. From this, they show how to recover theoretically this estimator from a optimization algorithm. Finally they expose cases where the designed estimator show some good generalization properties while the miminum norm interpolator does not.  The link with kernel methods is also made.
reviews.main_review:
**Overall comments and impression**

First, I would like to say that I am not an expert in statistical learning theory, so that I may have missed some insights in the results.

To begin, I would like to say that the paper is perfectly well written: the story is very clear and the mathematical writing is precise and fluid. This made the reading very pleasant and I would like to thank the authors for that.

The aim of the author is clear: provide an (under certain constraints) optimal linear predictor that may perform better than the minimum norm one naturally considered in the literature (naturally coming from GD or SGD on the empirical risk). The authors do so quite effortlessly ad give, to my opinion a nice result for those asking whether minimum norm solutions are always good estimators. **Yet**, I would have liked a lot more comments on the shape of this estimator: what intuition should the reader build when reading the paper ? A clear weakness is that the authors never really take the time of analysing why makes the estimator optimal (beyond technical considerations). In the very same manner, Proposition 2 gives that the optimal linear response is in fact the solution recovered by GD with a certain initialization: could the authors comment a bit on this ? 

The result on the divergence of the minimum norm linear interpolator (whereas the optimal one does not) is nice: however I would really need to understand further *why* and *when* we should expect this to happen. And in general cases *how close* should I expect the minimum norm interpolator to be close to the optimal one ? 


**Minor comments:**

- I guess that the part on the random matrix concepts arrive too early. As far as I understood, there is absolutely no need to put this part at the beginning, and this could be postpone in section 4.2 or even in the Appendix. 

- The part on random features model gives nothing new to the results: they are not commented (at all) and are absolutely expected once the linear model is covered. If these results are not further commented, then I recommend to put it in the Appendix as they do not give any additional insight on the paper. A nice introduction on why linear model are trendy is enough in my opinion (NTK, random features...)

- In Section 4.2, is this possible to derive a close form for the asymptotic risk of the optimal interpolator ? If not, why is this complicated ?

- Give more motivation and justification for the form of the *optimal linear interpolating estimator* you build. Why are you okay with the dependance on \Sigma, \phi, \delta. How could relax these ? I understand that these parameter will be estimated on the fly later: but once again, the comments on this are not deep precise enough to convince me. In particular there are only a few comments on the Graphiical Lasso approximation and why this is a good idea to approximate the population covariance.

- Plots commenting Proposition 2 would rely complement the result! If not, it seems not so important: a comparison with GD without preconditionning would be welcomed.


**Conclusion**

Even if I really enjoyed reading the paper, my opinion is that, under this form, the results lack from commentaries, intuition, insights and a bit of further developments.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper under reviews studies the problem of optimal linear interpolation. More precisely the authors derive an explicit formula for the *best* linear interpolator that can be written as a linear function of the outputs and as a function of desired quantities. From this, they show how to recover theoretically this estimator from a optimization algorithm. Finally they expose cases where the designed estimator show some good generalization properties while the miminum norm interpolator does not.  The link with kernel methods is also made.

And in general cases *how close* should I expect the minimum norm interpolator to be close to the optimal one ? 


**Minor comments:**

- I guess that the part on the random matrix concepts arrive too early. As far as I understood, there is absolutely no need to put this part at the beginning, and this could be postpone in section 4.2 or even in the Appendix. 

- The part on random features model gives nothing new to the results: they are not commented (at all) and are absolutely expected once the linear model is covered. If these results are not further commented, then I recommend to put it in the Appendix as they do not give any additional insight on the paper. A nice introduction on why linear model are trendy is enough in my opinion (NTK, random features...)

- In Section 4.2, is this possible to derive a close form for the asymptotic risk of the optimal interpolator ? If not, why is this complicated ?

- Give more motivation and justification for the form of the *optimal linear interpolating estimator* you build. Why are you okay with the dependance on \Sigma, \phi, \delta. How could relax these ? I understand that these parameter will be estimated on the fly later: but once again, the comments on this are not deep precise enough to convince me. In particular there are only a few comments on the Graphiical Lasso approximation and why this is a good idea to approximate the population covariance.

reviews.summary: This paper considers the problem of optimal interpolation in linear regression. It defines the class of response-linear achievable interpolators, and provides a closed-form expression for the optimal interpolator in this class. The paper is about an important topic in machine learning and statistics. However, it is unclear to me whether the "achievable" interpolators are really achievable, and as a result if the investigation is meaningful.
reviews.main_review:
Major comments and questions:
1. The paper claims that the realization of true data-generating parameter and the realization of the noise in the training data are unknown. However, it assumes that the population covariance, the signal-to-noise ratio, and the covariance of the prior for the signal are known. The latter seems to be more unrealistic. For high dimensional data, the covariance matrix contains much more number of parameters than the regression coefficient so it is a much harder problem to estimate the covariance matrix. In a Bayesian setting, the primary goal is to find the distribution of the parameter. If the distribution of the parameter is assumed to be known, then there is no estimation problem.

2. For $\Phi$, do you mean it is the correlation matrix? Otherwise calling $r^2$ the signal is not appropriate. 
   
3. The population risk between lines 125 and 126 is not clear.It is written as the conditional expectation given $X, \xi, w^*$, but this does not make sense. A risk should not be random.

4. I think the $y$ in line 131 should be $y_i$, but what is the $y$ in line 132? The $y$ in line 117 should be $y_i$? How about the $y$ in lines 120 and 124? Each of these may be typos or poor notations, but when there are too many this type of problems and makes it hard to following the paper it becomes a major issue. 
   
5. Line 142, I believe you need $\gamma\ge1$.

6. Line 156 says "rank(X) = n with probability 1" while this becomes a condition in line 160.

7. Definition 4, do you mean there exists a known function? Otherwise, the function $f$ may depends further on other unknowns.

8. As in my first point, the $w_O$ is not realistic. The updating formula in (7) requires the inverse of $\Sigma$ which is almost impossible for high dimensional data. 

9. I don't think Hastie et al. (2019) considered the case of $\Phi=I_d$. It is probably a typo here.
   

Minor issues and typos:

Line 53, "are best" to "are the best".

In (5), use paratheses to make the definition clear.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Please see the main review.
reviews.rating: 4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper considers the problem of optimal interpolation in linear regression. It defines the class of response-linear achievable interpolators, and provides a closed-form expression for the optimal interpolator in this class. The paper is about an important topic in machine learning and statistics. However, it is unclear to me whether the "achievable" interpolators are really achievable, and as a result if the investigation is meaningful.

Main Review:
Major comments and questions:
1. The paper claims that the realization of true data-generating parameter and the realization of the noise in the training data are unknown. However, it assumes that the population covariance, the signal-to-noise ratio, and the covariance of the prior for the signal are known. The latter seems to be more unrealistic. For high dimensional data, the covariance matrix contains much more number of parameters than the regression coefficient so it is a much harder problem to estimate the covariance matrix. In a Bayesian setting, the primary goal is to find the distribution of the parameter. If the distribution of the parameter is assumed to be known, then there is no estimation problem.

2. For $\Phi$, do you mean it is the correlation matrix? Otherwise calling $r^2$ the signal is not appropriate. 
   
3. The population risk between lines 125 and 126 is not clear.It is written as the conditional expectation given $X, \xi, w^*$, but this does not make sense. A risk should not be random.

4. I think the $y$ in line 131 should be $y_i$, but what is the $y$ in line 132? The $y$ in line 117 should be $y_i$? How about the $y$ in lines 120 and 124? Each of these may be typos or poor notations, but when there are too many this type of problems and makes it hard to following the paper it becomes a major issue. 
   
5. Line 142, I believe you need $\gamma\ge1$.

6. Line 156 says "rank(X) = n with probability 1" while this becomes a condition in line 160.

7. Definition 4, do you mean there exists a known function? Otherwise, the function $f$ may depends further on other unknowns.

8. As in my first point, the $w_O$ is not realistic. The updating formula in (7) requires the inverse of $\Sigma$ which is almost impossible for high dimensional data. 

9. I don't think Hastie et al. (2019) considered the case of $\Phi=I_d$. It is probably a typo here.
   

Minor issues and typos:

Line 53, "are best" to "are the best".

In (5), use paratheses to make the definition clear.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Please see the main review.

Rating:
4: Ok but not good enough - rejection

title: Differentiable rendering with perturbed optimizers

reviews.summary:
This paper proposes a new differentiable renderer by introducing perturbed optimizers. The paper re-writes the non-differentiable rasterization and aggregation steps to heaven side functions and relaxes them to be differentiable by adding noise. 
The author shows that previous work, especially softras, can be treated as a special case of the perturbed renderer with specific prior selections. The authors also demonstrated in several experiments they achieve better or comparable results than the state of the art.
reviews.main_review:
Strengths

1) Differentiable reformulation
This paper treats rasterization as a linear programing problem.  It extends the [4] to the rendering pipeline and relaxes the non-differentiable rendering to be differentiable by introducing perturbed optimizers. It provides a new differentiable renderer from a new perspective.

2) Better performance
The authors show AS and VR are very useful in optimization, for both perturbed renderer and softras. In the 6D pose comparison, the results are better than softras.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact:
There are several weak points of this paper.

1) The paper is not self-contained.  I have a hard time understanding each equation. While the authors largely rely on [4], it makes readers hard to follow without knowing [4]. Moreover, the notations in this paper are very vague.   E.g. line 66 it should be j \in [1,.., m]?  line 76 z should be depth order?  If it is depth, how do you compute <z, y>?  line 88, what's v?  The paper writing definitely needs to be improved.


2) Relation of softras and noise types.  The authors show that softras can be treated as the special case of the perturbed renderer. Softras has some hyperparameters which are hard to explain but in the new renderer, all the parameters have clear noise intensity meaning.  While this is very good, but the paper lacks further analysis of which noise should we use. E.g. in table 1, it seems Gaussian noise is the best. But could it be generalized to other cases? 


3) Comparison.  In line 214 the authors said, "Unless stated, the results for the other renderers are extracted from their original publications, as we were not able to get access to their implementations."  I am a little confused about this.  Clearly, N3mr, softras, dibr released their renderers. Thus, why not simply replace the renderer from perturbed renderer to new renderers and compare the score?  Directly copying scores from others' papers may have the wrong comparison. While all the methods use shapenet, the rendered images could vary from lighting to camera views. I would highly recommend the authors redo the comparison.


4)  The authors assume each pixel will be influenced by all the triangles. For each pixel, it renders the influences of all the triangles and combines them together(Eq 3).  I wonder what's the rendering speed of the perturbed renderer? Theoretically, such design plus the MC sampling will make the renderer very time-consuming.  The authors should show the rendering speed comparison.


5) Following the last concern.  Generally speaking, rasterization accelerates the rendering speed by assuming each pixel is influenced by the frontest triangle.  On the other side, MC is always used in ray tracing to compute multiple bounces. The authors introduce MC in rasterization, which seems to adopt the weak points of two rendering methods.   I wonder did the authors consider extending it to RT?

Post rebuttal:

The authors addressed my concerns and I will raise the score to be marginally above the threshold and be happy to see this paper get accepted.

Summary:
This paper proposes a new differentiable renderer by introducing perturbed optimizers. The paper re-writes the non-differentiable rasterization and aggregation steps to heaven side functions and relaxes them to be differentiable by adding noise. 
The author shows that previous work, especially softras, can be treated as a special case of the perturbed renderer with specific prior selections. The authors also demonstrated in several experiments they achieve better or comparable results than the state of the art.

Main Review:
Strengths

1) Differentiable reformulation
This paper treats rasterization as a linear programing problem.  It extends the [4] to the rendering pipeline and relaxes the non-differentiable rendering to be differentiable by introducing perturbed optimizers. It provides a new differentiable renderer from a new perspective.

2) Better performance
The authors show AS and VR are very useful in optimization, for both perturbed renderer and softras. In the 6D pose comparison, the results are better than softras.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
There are several weak points of this paper.

1) The paper is not self-contained.  I have a hard time understanding each equation. While the authors largely rely on [4], it makes readers hard to follow without knowing [4]. Moreover, the notations in this paper are very vague.   E.g. line 66 it should be j \in [1,.., m]?  line 76 z should be depth order?  If it is depth, how do you compute <z, y>?  line 88, what's v?  The paper writing definitely needs to be improved.


2) Relation of softras and noise types.  The authors show that softras can be treated as the special case of the perturbed renderer. Softras has some hyperparameters which are hard to explain but in the new renderer, all the parameters have clear noise intensity meaning.  While this is very good, but the paper lacks further analysis of which noise should we use. E.g. in table 1, it seems Gaussian noise is the best. But could it be generalized to other cases? 


3) Comparison.  In line 214 the authors said, "Unless stated, the results for the other renderers are extracted from their original publications, as we were not able to get access to their implementations."  I am a little confused about this.  Clearly, N3mr, softras, dibr released their renderers. Thus, why not simply replace the renderer from perturbed renderer to new renderers and compare the score?  Directly copying scores from others' papers may have the wrong comparison. While all the methods use shapenet, the rendered images could vary from lighting to camera views. I would highly recommend the authors redo the comparison.


4)  The authors assume each pixel will be influenced by all the triangles. For each pixel, it renders the influences of all the triangles and combines them together(Eq 3).  I wonder what's the rendering speed of the perturbed renderer? Theoretically, such design plus the MC sampling will make the renderer very time-consuming.  The authors should show the rendering speed comparison.


5) Following the last concern.  Generally speaking, rasterization accelerates the rendering speed by assuming each pixel is influenced by the frontest triangle.  On the other side, MC is always used in ray tracing to compute multiple bounces. The authors introduce MC in rasterization, which seems to adopt the weak points of two rendering methods.   I wonder did the authors consider extending it to RT?

Post rebuttal:

The authors addressed my concerns and I will raise the score to be marginally above the threshold and be happy to see this paper get accepted.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Expanded Review

Summary  
This paper introduces a novel differentiable rendering framework by leveraging perturbed optimizers to smooth the inherently non-differentiable rasterization and aggregation steps. The authors reformulate rasterization as a linear program (LP) and apply randomized smoothing to both the Heaviside-based occupancy computation (Eq. 2) and the Z-buffer aggregation (Eq. 3), yielding a perturbed renderer (Eqs. 8–10). They further show that previous methods such as SoftRas emerge as special cases under specific noise priors (Appendix A), propose a control-variate variance-reduction scheme for the resulting Monte Carlo estimators (Sec. 4.2), and introduce an adaptive smoothing schedule driven by sensitivity analysis (Sec. 4.3). Empirically, the perturbed renderer achieves improved 6D pose refinement on a colored cube as well as competitive or superior performance in single-view self-supervised 3D mesh reconstruction on ShapeNet.

Strengths  
1) Differentiable reformulation with rigorous grounding  
   - By casting rasterization and Z-buffering as argmax LPs (Eqs. 1–3), the paper extends the framework of perturbed optimizers [4] to the rendering pipeline.  
   - This fresh perspective unifies several existing differentiable renderers under the lens of randomized smoothing: for instance, choosing Gumbel noise reproduces the SoftRas formulation (Appendix A).  
   - The theoretical derivation of the Jacobian of the smoothed optimizer (Eq. 6) clarifies why the gradients are non-zero almost everywhere, addressing a key shortcoming of earlier first-order Taylor or manual interpolation proxies (e.g. OpenDR [24], NMR [16]).

2) Practical improvements: variance reduction and adaptive smoothing  
   - The control variate trick (Eqs. 13–14) drastically reduces the variance of the Monte Carlo gradient estimates: with as few as M = 2–8 samples, the perturbed renderer achieves stable convergence (Fig. 5 left), whereas the naive estimator would require tens of samples.  
   - The adaptive smoothing scheme (Eqs. 15–18) automatically decreases noise intensity as the loss becomes locally convex, avoiding tedious hyperparameter tuning of σ, γ. This mechanism boosts both the perturbed renderer and SoftRas in 6D pose refinement (Fig. 5 right).

3) Demonstrated state-of-the-art or competitive results  
   - On the toy 6D pose optimization of a colored cube (Sec. 5), the perturbed renderer with Gaussian smoothing solves over 90% of instances from 20° perturbations and outperforms SoftRas in both average error and success rate (Table 2).  
   - In self-supervised single-view mesh reconstruction on ShapeNet (Sec. 5), the method achieves a mean 3D IoU of 61.0%, exceeding SoftRas’s 60.0% and DIB-R’s 58.6% (Table 3). Qualitative outputs (Fig. 7) further attest to sharper, more accurate geometry and color recovery.

Limitations and Suggestions for Improvement  
1) Readability and self-containment  
   - The paper heavily relies on [4] and perturbed-optimizer concepts, making several derivations terse. For instance, in Eq. 5 the role of µ(z) and the normalization ν(z) could be elaborated with a brief intuitive recap, rather than presuming familiarity with [4].  
   - Notational ambiguities persist: in line 66 it should read “j ∈ [1,…,m]” for clarity; in line 76 the inner product “⟨z,y⟩” needs a short parenthetical explaining that z is the vector of inverse depths and y the occupancy weight; line 88 introduces “v” without defining it as the occupancy indicator vector. A pass focused on notation unification and explicit definitions would greatly aid comprehension.

2) Choice and analysis of noise distributions  
   - While the paper notes that Gaussian, Cauchy, or Gumbel priors yield different smoothing patterns (Sec. 4.1), it stops short of a systematic study of their relative merits across tasks. Table 2 shows Gaussian performing best on the cube, but it remains unclear under what conditions Cauchy or other heavy-tailed priors might be preferable. A small ablation over noise types on mesh reconstruction (e.g., Table 3) could clarify generalizability.

3) Comparison protocol and reproducibility  
   - The authors state (line 214) that they “could not access” official implementations of SoftRas and DIB-R, so they copied scores from original publications. However, differences in lighting, camera parameters, or silhouette thresholds can skew results. It would strengthen the claims to re-run all baselines under a unified pipeline (e.g., identical rendering settings in PyTorch3D) so that improvements are not attributable to incidental experimental mismatches.

4) Computational speed vs. overhead  
   - Since every pixel aggregates contributions from all m triangles via M Monte Carlo samples (Eqs. 8–10), the perturbed renderer’s per-frame cost may grow substantially with scene complexity. Table 1 reports forward/backward runtimes for a cube (m=12 triangles), but real-world meshes can have thousands of faces. A short discussion or benchmark on a higher-resolution mesh (e.g., m=500–1000) would help readers gauge the practical trade-offs compared to standard rasterization-based differentiable renderers.

5) Relation to ray tracing and multi-bounce effects  
   - The analysis in Sec. 1 emphasizes that rasterization limits computations to the frontmost triangle, whereas ray tracing employs Monte Carlo for global illumination. Introducing MC sampling into rasterization cleverly smooths discontinuities, yet it also inherits the potential inefficiency of path tracing. The paper could briefly discuss whether the perturbed renderer framework might be extended to full differentiable ray tracing (lines noted in Sec. 3), potentially combining benefits of both paradigms.

Minor Comments  
- Fig. 2 nicely visualizes the effect of increasing σ and γ, but adding a quantitative legend (e.g., exact σ values used) would make the figure more informative.  
- In Algorithm 1 (Appendix C), specifying the stopping criterion for tuning σ,γ (e.g., maximum iterations or threshold on sensitivity) would complete the description.  
- A short paragraph in the Conclusion contrasting the implicit regularization from noise (Sec. 4.3) with explicit regularizers in other renderers (e.g., Laplacian smoothness in mesh adaptation) would underscore an additional advantage of the proposed approach.

Decision  
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

Overall, this paper presents a well-motivated theoretical advance in differentiable rendering by importing perturbed optimizers, and backs it with solid empirical evidence. While improvements in clarity, baseline reproduction, and large-scale benchmarks would strengthen the work, the core contributions—particularly the variance reduction and adaptive smoothing—offer a valuable addition to the field. Given its novelty and demonstrated benefits, I recommend acceptance.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a new differentiable rendering method, differentiable perturbed renderers,  wherein the gradients of the rasterization step are approximated using perturbed optimizers. They implement this change on top of the SoftRas method and demonstrate that it leads to better pose estimation, and on par performance with state of the art methods in unsupervised single image 3d object reconstruction.
reviews.main_review:
This is a strong paper. It is written clearly, and the method is explained properly. Perturbed optimizers seem to be a new way for tackling optimization over discrete operations in deep learning, and their application to rasterization is well motivated and appropriate. The theory required for applying them to this new setting seems right, and is well explained. The results are also adequate for the claims made. It would have been nice to see higher performance on the reconstruction task, though diff rendering is a relatively new and so highlighting new avenues to explore , such as your proposed perturbed optimizers,  is important, and so I do not believe it is necessary.

 I would say one nice set of experiments to see would be optimization over the same set of parameters as in the DIB-R  paper Figure 2. ( camera position, texture , vertex positions, etc. ), with qualitative and quantitative comparisons to DIB-R and SoftRas. Having this would go a long was to increasing my score, and I believe would sell you paper quite a bit better. The pose estimation seems quite a bit easier than optimizing for texture and vertex position for example, and it would be nice to see how you method fairs, even if the performance is only on par or worse.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes.

reviews.summary:
This paper presents a method for converting a standard non-differentiable rasterization-based renderer into a differentiable counterpart using recent advances in the perturbed optimizer. The main contribution lies in the generic nature provided by the theoretical formulation, where some specifically chosen priors in the proposed framework can retrieve recent state-of-the-art differentiable renderers, e.g. SoftRas. 
As the proposed approach mainly relies on a Monte-Carlo estimator for generating the smoothing parameters. Variance-reduction mechanism and sensitivity analysis are also incorporated into the proposed framework to ensure the robustness of the approximate renderer. Experiments on pose estimation and single-view reconstruction are conducted for comparing the performance of the proposed method and previous state-of-the-art approaches.
reviews.main_review:
Pros:

- By introducing the perturbed optimizer to analyzing the non-differentiability of standard rasterization-based rendering pipeline provides new insight into constructing a generic differentiable renderer. The perturbed differentiable rendering can also be implemented on top of existing renderers without major modifications, which makes this approach versatile and a potential performance booster for existing DR techniques.

Cons:

- In terms of technical novelty, it seems to be a direct application of perturbed optimizer to differentiable rendering. Though it requires some efforts (such as the variance reduction and sensitivity analysis) to adapt the existing theory to this specific application, it is not very challenging as there are existing protocols to follow.

- Evaluation-wise, in the task of single-view reconstruction, though the proposed method can achieve comparable performance compared to the state-of-the-art approaches, it still fails to outperform DIB-R in mean accuracy and also outperformed by SoftRas in many categories, such as the Dresser, Car, etc. The 3D pose estimation is only evaluated on a simple cube which cannot provide a very comprehensive measurement of the proposed approach.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes

reviews.text:
Review for “Differentiable Rendering with Perturbed Optimizers”

Summary:
This paper addresses one of the long-standing challenges in 3D computer vision—making a rasterization-based renderer differentiable—by leveraging the framework of perturbed optimizers. The authors formulate the non-differentiable rasterization and aggregation steps as solutions to simple optimization problems (linear programs) and then apply randomized smoothing, in the form of perturbed optimizers, to obtain naturally differentiable renderers. They further introduce a variance-reduction mechanism using control variates and a sensitivity-based adaptive scheme for automatically tuning smoothing parameters during optimization. Experiments on single-view 6D pose estimation of a cube and self-supervised 3D mesh reconstruction from ShapeNet demonstrate that the perturbed renderer can match or exceed existing differentiable renderers such as SoftRas and DIB-R in certain metrics, while retaining a simple, modular implementation that can be applied on top of existing pipelines.

Strengths:
1. Novel theoretical perspective.  By viewing the non-smooth rasterization and Z-buffering steps as argmax problems over convex polytopes (Equations 2 and 3), the paper brings a new lens—perturbed optimizers—into differentiable rendering. This viewpoint unifies and generalizes previous approaches such as SoftRas and OpenDR, by showing that they correspond to particular noise priors (e.g., Gumbel or Logistic) in the smoothing framework (Appendix A).  
   - This insight deepens our understanding of why some smoothing schemes succeed or fail and highlights the versatility of the perturbed optimizer formulation.
   - It also suggests that one could experiment with other noise distributions (Gaussian, Cauchy, etc.) to better suit different rendering tasks or object geometries.

2. Practical variance reduction.  The authors recognize that naive Monte Carlo estimation of gradients (Equation 6) can be prohibitively noisy when the perturbation magnitude σ is small. By deriving a control-variate–based estimator (Equations 13 and 14) that exploits the zero-mean property of the score function ν(Z), they show in Figure 5-left that accurate 6D pose recovery from a 20° initial rotation requires only 2 samples instead of 32–64, offering a large computational saving.  
   - Table 1 further confirms that forward and backward passes with M=8 samples remain within the same order of magnitude (milliseconds and memory) as SoftRas or a hard renderer.

3. Adaptive smoothing via sensitivity analysis.  Section 4.3 and Equation 18 introduce an elegant mechanism to track the sensitivity ∂L/∂γ of the loss L with respect to the smoothing parameter γ. Since this sensitivity is provably positive near a local optimum under a convex RGB or silhouette loss (Equation 17), the method steadily reduces γ and σ, sharpening the gradients as convergence nears (Figure 5-right).  
   - This alleviates the need for manual tuning of smoothing schedules, a common pain point in existing differentiable renderers.

4. Empirical validation on two key tasks.  
   - On 6D pose estimation (Table 2 and Figure 6), the perturbed renderer with Gaussian noise outperforms SoftRas (which implicitly uses Gumbel smoothing) in terms of the percentage of solved tasks (errors <10°) across a range of initial perturbations.  
   - On self-supervised single-view mesh reconstruction (Table 3, Figure 7), the method achieves competitive IoU across 13 ShapeNet categories, demonstrating stable performance with low variance over multiple random seeds. Qualitative meshes and colorizations further confirm the practical utility of the supervisory signals from perturbed rendering.

5. Ease of integration and modularity.  The implementation, built on PyTorch3D, requires only minor modifications to existing rasterization-based pipelines. Researchers using SoftRas, DIB-R or similar frameworks could switch to perturbed rendering by plugging in a noise distribution and the control variate estimator.

Weaknesses:
1. Limited technical novelty beyond applying perturbation theory.  
   - Though the paper makes a compelling case for perturbed optimizers in rendering, the core idea is a fairly direct application of recent smoothing protocols (Berthet et al. 2020). The additional contributions—variance reduction via control variates and sensitivity-based adaptive schedules—are important but follow established methodologies in stochastic optimization (Section 4.2 and 4.3).  
   - In other words, adapting the general perturbed-optimizer machinery to this rendering context is well executed but not especially challenging given the existing literature.

2. Evaluation scope could be broader and deeper.  
   - The 6D pose estimation experiments focus primarily on a simple colored cube model (Figure 4), which limits insight into performance on complex, real-world objects with occlusions and non-uniform textures.  
   - In the single-view reconstruction task, although the perturbed renderer matches or exceeds SoftRas and rivals DIB-R in many classes (Table 3), it fails to outperform DIB-R in mean IoU overall and is outpaced by SoftRas on categories like Dresser and Car. This suggests that there may still be room for optimizing noise priors or adaptive schedules per class.  
   - A more comprehensive benchmark—for instance, using the LINEMOD dataset for pose estimation or more challenging multi-object scenes—would strengthen the empirical claims.

3. Lack of discussion on computational trade-offs in depth.  
   - While Table 1 quantifies forward/backward times and memory for various sample counts, the manuscript does not fully discuss how these translate to training throughput in large-scale settings (e.g., batch sizes, distributed training).  
   - It would be valuable to know at what batch size the memory overhead of M=8 samples becomes restrictive compared to deterministic approximations like SoftRas.

4. Hyperparameter sensitivity.  
   - Although the adaptive smoothing scheme reduces manual tuning, the algorithm still relies on initial choices for σ₀, γ₀ and the decay rate βγ. Figure 6-bottom suggests robustness to these initial values, but comprehensive ablations on βγ and decay schedules are missing.

Overall, this paper offers an insightful theoretical framing and demonstrates that perturbed optimizers can serve as a versatile, modular tool for differentiable rendering. The practical enhancements—variance reduction and adaptive smoothing—lead to strong empirical results on standard tasks, albeit with some limitations in object complexity and performance consistency across categories.

Recommendation:
Given the solid theoretical contributions, the practical utility of the method, and the overall positive empirical findings—balanced against modest technical novelty and a somewhat narrow experimental scope—I assign a rating of 6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold. The paper is likely to stimulate further research into stochastic methods for differentiable rendering and could serve as a drop-in improvement for existing pipelines.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a differentiable rendering method that builds connection between the 3D model and 2D perspective appearance. Different from previous methods, the differentiable rendering is formulated into an optimization problem. Two computer vision tasks, including pose estimation and 3D shape from color image, are evaluated with the proposed renderer and compared to SoftRas [22].
reviews.main_review:
Strength:
- The performance for pose estimation outperforms existing methods.
- The formulation seems novel.

Weakness:
- Could the proposed formulation be applied to other 3D shape representations, e.g. point cloud, implicit? If so, an experiment should be added. If not, then better to mention this is a mesh-specific technic.

- The algorithm seems to be computational costy, e.g. 1 min per image. Would this heavily affect the training efficiency? While comparing to other methods, e.g. SoftRas, the run-time efficiency and hyper-parameters, e.g. number of iteration, training epoch, should be mentioned. Otherwise the comparison may not be on a fair basis.

- Keeping the boundary differentiable is one of the main issues in existing differentiable rendering technics, which is also argued mainly in this paper. However it is not clear how this is improved by the proposed method. From Fig 2 and 4, it seems the boundary area has obvious artifacts. Also some experiments should be performed to highlight the improvements over occlusion boundary.

- The performance on 3D shape prediction does not outperform existing approaches. 

Overall, despite a novel formulation, the improvements over existing approaches are not clear. As shown in the experiment, the propose method does not necessarily produce more accurate rendering or support better for vision tasks. Considering that the proposed tech is also quite slow, the overall advantage over existing approaches is not very clear.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: No comment.
reviews.rating: 4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a differentiable rendering method that builds connection between the 3D model and 2D perspective appearance. Different from previous methods, the differentiable rendering is formulated into an optimization problem. Two computer vision tasks, including pose estimation and 3D shape from color image, are evaluated with the proposed renderer and compared to SoftRas [22].

Main Review:
Strength:
- The performance for pose estimation outperforms existing methods.
- The formulation seems novel.

Weakness:
- Could the proposed formulation be applied to other 3D shape representations, e.g. point cloud, implicit? If so, an experiment should be added. If not, then better to mention this is a mesh-specific technic.

- The algorithm seems to be computational costy, e.g. 1 min per image. Would this heavily affect the training efficiency? While comparing to other methods, e.g. SoftRas, the run-time efficiency and hyper-parameters, e.g. number of iteration, training epoch, should be mentioned. Otherwise the comparison may not be on a fair basis.

- Keeping the boundary differentiable is one of the main issues in existing differentiable rendering technics, which is also argued mainly in this paper. However it is not clear how this is improved by the proposed method. From Fig 2 and 4, it seems the boundary area has obvious artifacts. Also some experiments should be performed to highlight the improvements over occlusion boundary.

- The performance on 3D shape prediction does not outperform existing approaches. 

Overall, despite a novel formulation, the improvements over existing approaches are not clear. As shown in the experiment, the propose method does not necessarily produce more accurate rendering or support better for vision tasks. Considering that the proposed tech is also quite slow, the overall advantage over existing approaches is not very clear.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
No comment.

Rating:
4: Ok but not good enough - rejection

title: NAS-Bench-x11 and the Power of Learning Curves

reviews.summary:
This paper proposes another benchmark dataset for NAS, NAS-Bench-x11. Compared to the original benchmarks NAS-Bench-101, NAS-Bench-301 and NAS-Bench-NLP, NAS-Bench-x11 contains the full training
information for each candidate architecture especially the learning curves so that it allows multi-fidelity NAS algorithms

NAS-Bench-x11 is a surrogate benchmark, which trains a surrogate model to predict the learning curve of the vast amount of candidate architectures. Prediction of the learning curve can be accomplished by singular value decomposition and noise modeling.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:

The paper is well motivated and clearly written. The paper is clear about limitations too.

I think this paper will be valuable to the community.  The created NAS-bench-x11 contains the full training information of different architectures, which may make it more convenient to analyse NAS algorithms and promote further NAS algorithm design. The idea of predicting the learning curve is very interesting to me. 

The authors conducted extensive experiments to demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method.  

Weakness:

This paper is a completion of the previously created benchmarks. Providing learning curves is also considered before in NAS-Bench-201, which weakens the novelty of this paper. So I recommend a weak acceptence.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes

reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper introduces NAS-Bench-x11, a family of three surrogate benchmarks—NAS-Bench-111, NAS-Bench-311, and NAS-Bench-NLP11—that extend the popular NAS-Bench-101, NAS-Bench-301, and NAS-Bench-NLP benchmarks by providing the complete learning curve information (train/validation/test losses and accuracies at every epoch) for each candidate architecture. The key technical contribution is a two-stage surrogate modeling approach: first, the authors apply singular value decomposition (SVD) to compress high-dimensional learning curves into a low-rank representation, train a regression model (e.g., LightGBM) to predict these compressed coefficients from architecture encodings (and in some cases partial observed accuracies), and then reconstruct the mean learning curve via the SVD decoder; second, they augment these predictions with a noise model—ranging from simple per-epoch standard deviations to Gaussian kernel density estimates—to sample realistic noisy curves. The paper also demonstrates how this full-curve surrogate enables the direct application of multi-fidelity NAS techniques, such as learning curve extrapolation (LCE) and successive halving, and provides a simple framework to convert single-fidelity NAS algorithms (regularized evolution, local search, BANANAS) into multi-fidelity versions by early-stopping unpromising architectures based on partial-curve extrapolation.

Strengths:
1. Strong Motivation and Clear Writing: The authors clearly motivate the need for full-curve information on large NAS benchmarks, pointing out that while NAS-Bench-201 provides per-epoch data for a small search space (6,466 unique architectures), NAS-Bench-101 and NAS-Bench-301 only supply sparse or final-epoch accuracies. This gap has hindered multi-fidelity methods in NAS research, despite their success in hyperparameter optimization. The paper is well structured—Section 3 details the surrogate construction and Section 4 lays out the LCE framework—making the methodology easy to follow.
2. Methodological Novelty in Surrogate Construction: The use of truncated SVD for dimensionality reduction of learning curves is an elegant choice. As shown in Figure 2 (left), the first six singular values capture most of the variance in NAS-Bench-101 curves, and Figure 2 (middle) demonstrates that k = 6 achieves the lowest reconstruction MSE without overfitting noise. The subsequent training of LightGBM to predict these six coefficients from one-hot adjacency encodings, plus optionally the tabular accuracies at epochs {4, 12, 36, 108}, achieves strong R² and Kendall Tau correlations (Table 2 reports an average KT of 0.611 and final-epoch KT of 0.794 for NAS-Bench-111 with added accuracies). The noise models, especially the Gaussian KDE on residuals, further align the surrogate’s output distribution with ground truth, yielding low KL divergence (1.710 on average for NAS-Bench-111) and a realistic rate of “spike anomalies.”
3. Comprehensive Evaluation of Surrogates: The authors rigorously assess the surrogates on held-out test sets using multiple metrics: coefficient of determination (R²), Kendall Tau rank correlation (KT) both averaged over all epochs and at the final epoch, KL divergence between predicted and actual noisy-curve distributions, and the proportion of curves where the maximum accuracy exceeds the final accuracy by more than the 95th percentile (the “spike anomaly” test). This multi-pronged evaluation gives confidence in the surrogate’s fidelity before users adopt it for NAS studies.
4. Demonstration of Downstream Utility: In Section 5, the paper applies the new benchmarks to benchmark 12 NAS algorithms—4 single-fidelity methods (random search, regularized evolution, local search, BANANAS), 2 multi-armed bandit methods (Hyperband, BOHB), and 6 learning-curve-extrapolation-enhanced variants (each of REA, LS, BANANAS augmented with WPM [8] or LcSVR [1]). Figures 3 and 4 show that the LcSVR-augmented algorithms consistently outperform their single-fidelity counterparts and even surpass Hyperband and BOHB on multiple search spaces. For instance, BANANAS-SVR achieves the lowest validation regret on NAS-Bench-311, and LS-SVR is top on NAS-Bench-201 CIFAR-10. These results illustrate the practical benefits of having full learning curves.
5. Open-Source Release and Reproducibility: The authors follow best practices by releasing code, pretrained surrogate models, and detailed training pipelines at https://github.com/automl/nas-bench-x11. They also adhere to the NAS best practices checklist and provide appendices on model hyperparameters, ablation studies (e.g., choosing Efew ≈ 20% Emax for LCE), and training details.

Weakness:
1. Incremental Novelty Over Prior Benchmarks: Although NAS-Bench-x11 offers a clear advantage by supplying full learning curves on large search spaces, the idea of recording or predicting per-epoch performance was already explored in NAS-Bench-201 (which provides full curves for ∼6.5k architectures) and in surrogate benchmarks that model final-epoch accuracy. This paper thus reads as a systematic completion and extension of existing benchmarks rather than a wholly new conceptual breakthrough. The main novelty lies in the combination of SVD-based compression with noise modeling, but the overall framework parallels prior work on tabular and surrogate NAS benchmarks.
2. Dependency on Surrogate Quality: While the authors report strong correlation metrics, certain settings—especially NAS-Bench-NLP11—rely on training each architecture for three epochs before querying the surrogate to achieve an average KT of 0.862. This requirement reduces the theoretical speedup (∼15× rather than orders of magnitude). For very noisy search spaces or new domains, surrogate accuracy may degrade, making multi-fidelity gains less reliable without additional seed training.
3. Limited Discussion of Domain Generalization: The benchmarks cover CIFAR-10/DARTS, CIFAR-10/DARTS-301, and Penn Treebank for language modeling, but it remains unclear how easily the surrogate-construction approach scales to other tasks (e.g., ImageNet-scale search spaces, reinforcement learning policies) with more complex noise characteristics. A brief discussion on extending to future domains would strengthen the manuscript.

Overall, the paper is well written, thoroughly evaluated, and likely to be of high value to the NAS community by making multi-fidelity methods more accessible. The technical contributions in surrogate design and the demonstration of downstream gains justify publication. I therefore recommend a weak acceptance (rating 6: marginally above the acceptance threshold).

reviews.summary: Existing NAS benchmarks often only include the architecture performance at the final or some selected checkpoint epochs. However, per-epoch information is essential for developing and evaluating multi-fidelity NAS algorithms. This work proposed to fit a surrogate model to predict the learning curve given an architecture encoding. Utilizing the proposed predictor, the author extends three existing benchmarks NASBench-101, NASBench-301, and NASBench-NLP to include per-epoch accuracies. Further, to demonstrate the necessity of developing multi-fidelity algorithms for NAS, the author also describes a procedure to extend the single-fidelity algorithms to multi-fidelity ones by using learning curve extrapolation to filter out candidate proposals with partial training. Despite possible room for improvements on the performance of the learning curve predictor used, the proposed method would enable researchers to develop and rapidly test multi-fidelity NAS algorithms. I vote for acceptance.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:
1. The contribution of this paper is relevant to the NAS community and would accelerate research on multi-fidelity NAS algorithms.
2. The performance of the proposed learning curve predictor model is overall satisfactory.
3. The author adopted the proposed method to extend three existing benchmarks to include per-epoch information.

Weakness:
1. The author reported ranking correlation and KL divergence between the predicted learning curve and ground truth. The numbers seem reasonable, but the errors are still non-negligible. It is unclear how misleading these errors would be if someone tries to develop multi-fidelity algorithms by querying the proposed extended benchmark. How would the existing multi-fidelity NAS algorithms perform under the proposed benchmark, over ground-truth data? This might be doable for NASBench201 or NASBench111 (the version created without using information about the given 4 checkpoint epochs), as they provide ground-truth accuracies on at least some checkpoints?

Comments and Questions:
1. For NASBench-NLP, using the accuracy from the first few epochs as features significantly improves the learning curve predictor's performance. Can we also expect visible improvements on other spaces, if the info of the first few epochs is also provided?

2. You mentioned in para 355 that future surrogate works should save full learning curve info to avoid the need for any extra extensions. But surrogate benchmarks like NASBench-301 only train a small portion of the architectures fully, and the large majority part of the search space will be benched by the predictor. So it seems that such surrogate benchmark would still need to be extended with learning curve prediction anyway. Could you elaborate more on this?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes

Summary:
Existing NAS benchmarks often only include the architecture performance at the final or some selected checkpoint epochs. However, per-epoch information is essential for developing and evaluating multi-fidelity NAS algorithms. This work proposed to fit a surrogate model to predict the learning curve given an architecture encoding. Utilizing the proposed predictor, the author extends three existing benchmarks NASBench-101, NASBench-301, and NASBench-NLP to include per-epoch accuracies. Further, to demonstrate the necessity of developing multi-fidelity algorithms for NAS, the author also describes a procedure to extend the single-fidelity algorithms to multi-fidelity ones by using learning curve extrapolation to filter out candidate proposals with partial training. Despite possible room for improvements on the performance of the learning curve predictor used, the proposed method would enable researchers to develop and rapidly test multi-fidelity NAS algorithms. I vote for acceptance.

Main Review:
Strength:
1. The contribution of this paper is relevant to the NAS community and would accelerate research on multi-fidelity NAS algorithms.
2. The performance of the proposed learning curve predictor model is overall satisfactory.
3. The author adopted the proposed method to extend three existing benchmarks to include per-epoch information.

Weakness:
1. The author reported ranking correlation and KL divergence between the predicted learning curve and ground truth. The numbers seem reasonable, but the errors are still non-negligible. It is unclear how misleading these errors would be if someone tries to develop multi-fidelity algorithms by querying the proposed extended benchmark. How would the existing multi-fidelity NAS algorithms perform under the proposed benchmark, over ground-truth data? This might be doable for NASBench201 or NASBench111 (the version created without using information about the given 4 checkpoint epochs), as they provide ground-truth accuracies on at least some checkpoints?

Comments and Questions:
1. For NASBench-NLP, using the accuracy from the first few epochs as features significantly improves the learning curve predictor's performance. Can we also expect visible improvements on other spaces, if the info of the first few epochs is also provided?

2. You mentioned in para 355 that future surrogate works should save full learning curve info to avoid the need for any extra extensions. But surrogate benchmarks like NASBench-301 only train a small portion of the architectures fully, and the large majority part of the search space will be benched by the predictor. So it seems that such surrogate benchmark would still need to be extended with learning curve prediction anyway. Could you elaborate more on this?

Yes

reviews.summary: The authors describe a mechanism to predict learning curves which they use to generate surrogate benchmarks from existing NAS-Bench versions without learning curves. The authors explore different ways to create learning curve embeddings, different ways to predict these embeddings using the architecture representation as an input and different ways to model the training noise. Furthermore, the authors discuss a short study on using learning curve extrapolation in context of speeding-up NAS.
reviews.main_review:
The authors provide a good description of their method, alternative ways to solve each problem faced and support their decisions with empirical evidence.

My main concern is about the usefulness of this surrogate benchmark which has problems as partly discussed by the authors. It remains unclear what impact these problems would have.

The noise modelling in this paper leads to artifacts we would not see in real learning curves. First, it is very unlikely that the noise is architecture- and hyperparameter-independent. Second, given just the very few examples in Figure 1 we observe that the predicted learning curve in early epochs can already exceed to highest value of the entire learning curve (possibly due to very high noise levels).
In general, all surrogate benchmarks have the problem that they introduce some sort of bias which will not match what you would see in reality. Therefore, these datasets are more similar to synthetic datasets than real ones. They might be nice to prototype but I wouldn’t use them to evaluate NAS methods.

The experiment on combining learning curve extrapolation is a nice reminder for the NAS community to keep these approaches in mind when comparing different approaches. However, it provides no novelty since these methods have been used in the context of NAS already (see [1]).
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes
reviews.confidence: 5: You are absolutely certain about your assessment. You are very familiar with the related work and checked the math/other details carefully.

Furthermore, the authors discuss a short study on using learning curve extrapolation in context of speeding-up NAS.

reviews.text:
Summary:  
The manuscript introduces a methodology to generate full learning curves for neural architectures in existing NAS benchmarks (e.g., NAS-Bench-101, NAS-Bench-301, NAS-Bench-NLP) that originally only provided limited or final-epoch information. The authors employ singular value decomposition (SVD) to compress noisy learning curves into low-dimensional representations, train surrogate regressors (LightGBM, XGBoost, or MLP) on these compressed coefficients, and then reconstruct full curves via the inverse transformation. To simulate training noise, they fit simple Gaussian-based models—including a global standard-deviation model, a Gaussian kernel density estimate (GKDE), and a sliding-window standard-deviation approach. They evaluate these surrogate benchmarks (NAS-Bench-111, NAS-Bench-311, NAS-Bench-NLP11) using metrics such as average and final-epoch R², Kendall Tau (KT) rank correlation, Kullback–Leibler (KL) divergence, and spike-anomaly rates. Finally, they demonstrate a framework that plugs learning curve extrapolation (LCE) methods (WPM [8] and LcSVR [1]) into popular single-fidelity NAS algorithms (regularized evolution, local search, BANANAS), yielding multi-fidelity variants that can stop unpromising architectures early.

Strengths and Positive Aspects:  
- The paper is well structured and provides a clear, step-by-step description of the surrogate-creation pipeline: from gathering partial learning curves to SVD-based compression (Fig. 2) to surrogate model selection and noise-modeling strategies.  
- The authors conduct a comprehensive ablation study spanning two compression methods (SVD vs. VAE), three surrogate regressors (LGB, XGB, MLP), and three noise models (stddev, GKDE, sliding-window), totaling eighteen configurations (Appendix C). They report detailed evaluations on held-out test sets (Table 2), showing that SVD + LightGBM with GKDE or sliding-window noise achieves the best KT and KL scores.  
- By extending NAS-Bench-101 to NAS-Bench-111 (1,101 fully trained architectures with full curves), NAS-Bench-301 to NAS-Bench-311 (66 k training points covering the 10¹⁸ DARTS space), and NAS-Bench-NLP to NAS-Bench-NLP11 (14 k points over 10⁵³ search space), they bridge an important gap in enabling multi-fidelity methods.  
- The LCE framework is clearly explained (Algorithms 1–2) and empirically validated across four search spaces, confirming that LCE-augmented variants (especially with LcSVR) consistently outperform their single-fidelity counterparts (Figs. 3–4). This demonstration underlines the potential speedups and improved regret curves achievable by extrapolating learning curves.

Main Concerns and Critique Points (all preserved from the original review):  
1. **Questionable Practical Usefulness of the Surrogate Benchmarks**  
   While the surrogate datasets enable rapid CPU-based experimentation, their utility for accurately evaluating NAS methods in realistic GPU-based settings remains unclear. The authors acknowledge that surrogate benchmarks inevitably introduce biases and cannot fully capture real-world training dynamics. For example, Table 2 shows average KT values in the 0.7–0.9 range, which—although respectable—imply significant rank-order noise compared to real multi-seed evaluations. It is not demonstrated how such surrogate-induced biases translate into misleading conclusions when comparing NAS algorithms in practice. One might overfit methods to the idiosyncrasies of the surrogate rather than to true training behavior.

2. **Simplistic, Architecture-Independent Noise Modeling Leads to Artifacts**  
   The choice to assume isotropic Gaussian noise that is either global or locally iid over sliding windows simplifies learning-curve variability but ignores architecture- and hyperparameter-dependent heteroscedasticity. In real training scenarios, certain architectures exhibit higher variance (e.g., deeper networks or those with many residual connections), and this variance evolves non-stationarily over epochs. As noted in Figure 1, some predicted curves exhibit “spikes” or early-epoch overshoots where validation accuracy temporarily exceeds any later value—an artifact of high global noise levels. Such anomalies (measured via the 5% spike-anomaly statistic described in Section 3.2) can mislead a NAS algorithm into prematurely selecting architectures with illusory early performance gains.

3. **Surrogates Resemble Synthetic Datasets More Than Real Ones**  
   Because the surrogate benchmarks rely on fitted models rather than true training data, they behave more like synthetic benchmarks. While they are useful for rapid prototyping and reproducibility, their predictive validity for real NAS pipelines is limited. The differences in domain shift between surrogate and real accuracy distributions, as evidenced by non-negligible KL divergences and KT discrepancies (even at final epochs), raise concerns about the generalizability of conclusions drawn from these benchmarks.

4. **Limited Novelty in Combining LCE with NAS Algorithms**  
   The experiment showing that learning curve extrapolation can speed up NAS is a helpful reminder, but it lacks conceptual novelty. The idea of using multi-fidelity and curve-extrapolation techniques in NAS has been explored in prior work (e.g., Freeze-Thaw BayesOpt [63], WPM [8], LcSVR [1], bandit-based methods [35,14]). The authors’ contribution lies more in packaging these approaches under a unified façade and demonstrating empirical gains on their surrogate benchmarks, rather than devising fundamentally new extrapolation or bandit strategies.

Additional Observations and Suggestions:  
- It would strengthen the paper to investigate **heteroscedastic noise models**, where per-architecture variance is learned (e.g., via per-architecture variance heads in a Bayesian neural net). This could mitigate unrealistic artifacts like early overshoots and yield more faithful curve distributions.  
- A detailed **sensitivity analysis** of how surrogate biases affect the relative ranking of NAS algorithms on real hardware would help validate the benchmarks’ reliability. For instance, comparing method rankings on NAS-Bench-111 vs. on a small GPU-based run of the same architectures could reveal systematic distortions.  
- Although the authors provide a thorough **rank-correlation and KL** evaluation, they might also report **calibration** metrics—e.g., how well predicted confidence intervals match empirical residuals—particularly for the GKDE and sliding-window noise models (cf. Figure 1’s 90% regions).  
- The surrogate benchmarks’ versions and associated code are made available, but the paper would benefit from a **clearer guideline** on how future researchers should report which surrogate version and which noise model they used, to ensure consistency and reproducibility.  
- In the LCE framework (Alg. 2), exploring **adaptive** Efew schedules—where the number of warm-up epochs for extrapolation is adjusted per architecture—could further boost efficiency, especially in heterogeneous search spaces like NLP11 where early accuracy is highly predictive.

Decision:  
5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold

Given the strengths in methodological clarity, comprehensive ablation, and the practical value of CPU-speedup benchmarks, this work makes a solid empirical contribution. However, significant doubts remain about the realism and generalizability of the surrogate benchmarks, and the novelty of the LCE integration is limited. As a result, I recommend the paper as marginally below acceptance.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The authors describe a mechanism to predict learning curves which they use to generate surrogate benchmarks from existing NAS-Bench versions without learning curves. The authors explore different ways to create learning curve embeddings, different ways to predict these embeddings using the architecture representation as an input and different ways to model the training noise. Furthermore, the authors discuss a short study on using learning curve extrapolation in context of speeding-up NAS.

Main Review:
The authors provide a good description of their method, alternative ways to solve each problem faced and support their decisions with empirical evidence.

My main concern is about the usefulness of this surrogate benchmark which has problems as partly discussed by the authors. It remains unclear what impact these problems would have.

The noise modelling in this paper leads to artifacts we would not see in real learning curves. First, it is very unlikely that the noise is architecture- and hyperparameter-independent. Second, given just the very few examples in Figure 1 we observe that the predicted learning curve in early epochs can already exceed to highest value of the entire learning curve (possibly due to very high noise levels).
In general, all surrogate benchmarks have the problem that they introduce some sort of bias which will not match what you would see in reality. Therefore, these datasets are more similar to synthetic datasets than real ones. They might be nice to prototype but I wouldn’t use them to evaluate NAS methods.

The experiment on combining learning curve extrapolation is a nice reminder for the NAS community to keep these approaches in mind when comparing different approaches. However, it provides no novelty since these methods have been used in the context of NAS already (see [1]).

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Yes

Rating:
5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold

reviews.main_review:
## Positive points:
1. The paper presents a method to extend the existing single-fidelity benchmarks to multi-fidelity ones with full training information.
2. The authors create NAS-Bench-111, NAS-Bench-311, and NAS-Bench-NLP11 benchmark datasets, which may be beneficial to develop new NAS algorithm.

## Negative points:
1. Does the proposed method apply to real world NAS application? In practice, given a dataset, we may have no available training information of any architectures. In this case, how to apply the proposed method? It would be limited if the proposed method is only able to extend the existing NAS benchmarks.
2. In line 220, why do the authors sample architectures from BANANAS, local search and regularized evolution?
3. In line 225, the authors mention that the method is able to improve the performance with the accuracies from the tabular benchmark (at epochs 4, 12, 36, 108) as additional features. More details of this should be provided.

title: Compressed Video Contrastive Learning

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a self-supervised method for compressed video representation learning, which helps save both storage and computation.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:

- The proposed method directly uses RGB and motion vectors in the compressed video for efficient representation learning.
- Experimental results show that the proposed method reduces the total (inference) time and storage, and achieves marginal improvement compared to state-of-the-art methods.

Weakness:

- Adding temporal learning objectives such as changing the temporal order of RGB frames or motion vectors within a clip ( as in IMRNet ), which is missing from the proposed method, would greatly help learning video representation.
- Modeling of the proposed method mostly applies the InfoNCE loss, only selecting of positive and negative samples are updated for RGB and motion vectors.
- As shown in table 1, the computation overhead largely comes from the preprocessing time. Though not an apple-to-apple comparison, parallel re-encoding for IMRNet may largely mitigate the overhead?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: The reviewer would encourage the authors to discuss limitations and impacts of the proposed method, as mentioned in the 'Main Review'.

Main Review:
Strength:

Weakness:

- Modeling of the proposed method mostly applies the InfoNCE loss, only selecting of positive and negative samples are updated for RGB and motion vectors.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Expanded Review

Summary  
This paper proposes a novel Motion Vector based Cross Guidance Contrastive Learning (MVCGC) framework for self-supervised video representation learning directly on compressed videos. By decoding RGB frames and motion vectors on-the-fly from standard video codecs (e.g., H.264/HEVC), the method avoids costly optical flow computation and large intermediate storage. A multi-instance InfoNCE objective with cross-guidance mining allows the RGB and motion vector streams to supervise each other, yielding representations that are both discriminative and efficient. Comprehensive experiments on UCF101 and HMDB51 demonstrate significant reductions in preprocessing time (from over 10 seconds per video to under 0.01s) and storage requirements (from tens of gigabytes to under 2 GB), while matching or slightly surpassing state-of-the-art methods using optical flow.

Strengths  
(1) On-the-fly decoding. The authors leverage “pyav”/FFmpeg to directly decode RGB frames and motion vector streams from compressed videos, without re-encoding or storing residuals. This design is clearly illustrated in Figure 2(c) and Table 1, where MVCGC’s preprocessing time per video is only 0.008 s compared to 12.7 s for IIC or 0.128 s for IMRNet. The efficient data-loading pipeline is well motivated and directly addresses the scalability challenge in large-scale SSVRL.  

(2) Cross-guidance contrastive learning. By constructing positive and negative pairs across RGB and motion-vector views, the model captures mutual information between appearance and motion cues. The multi-instance InfoNCE formulation (Eqs. 2–5) that mines top-k hard positives further enriches supervision beyond a single positive/negative. Ablation studies in Table 3 confirm that choosing k = 5 and requiring an initialization stage both improve UCF101 linear-probe accuracy by over 6%. The algorithmic novelty is well documented and supported by pseudo-code (Alg. 1) in the appendix.

(3) Empirical validation. The paper provides an extensive set of experiments on two downstream tasks (action recognition and retrieval). On UCF101 fully fine-tuning, MVCGC achieves 87.4% top-1 (Table 1), outperforming CoCLR (87.3%) while using only 1.9 GB storage compared to 35.4 GB. Retrieval results in Table 5 show new state-of-the-art recall@k on both UCF101 and HMDB51. Visualizations in Figure 4 further demonstrate that motion-vector features learned via MVCGC attend to semantically relevant motion patterns nearly as well as optical-flow features.

(4) Generality and reproducibility. MVCGC supports various modern codecs without re-encoding, in contrast to IMRNet’s reliance on MPEG-4 Part 2. The detailed implementation (S3D backbone, momentum queue, hyperparameters) is clearly described, which should facilitate reproduction and future extensions.

Weaknesses and Suggestions  
(1) Lack of explicit temporal pretext tasks. As noted, the current framework relies solely on the InfoNCE loss, without additional temporal objectives such as clip-order verification or playback speed prediction (e.g., as in IMRNet [Yu et al., 2021] or Yao et al., 2020). Incorporating a simple ordering or speed prediction task on RGB or motion-vector sequences could further strengthen temporal modeling, possibly raising recognition accuracy on fine-grained actions.

(2) Reliance on InfoNCE only. The novelty primarily lies in the sampling strategy across two views; the representation learning backbone and loss function remain standard contrastive. While the multi-instance extension is a meaningful enhancement, the modeling still applies only variants of InfoNCE. Exploring other objectives (e.g., clustering losses, prototype-based methods) could deepen the contribution.

(3) Preprocessing overhead comparison. Table 1 shows that MVCGC’s main advantage is in drastically reduced preprocessing time. However, the comparison to IMRNet is not strictly apple-to-apple—IMRNet could parallelize its re-encoding and residual computation across multiple CPU threads or machines, which might narrow the gap. A more detailed analysis of how GPU/CPU parallelism affects all methods would strengthen the claim that MVCGC is uniformly more efficient.

(4) Codec and quality variation. Although the paper mentions MVCGC works with various codecs (H.264, HEVC) and different motion-vector quantization settings, the experiments focus mainly on default codec configurations. An additional ablation on vector field granularity, GOP size, or compression quality would clarify how robust the method is to real-world video storage variations.

Limitations and Societal Impact  
The proposed MVCGC framework is designed for efficiency and could enable large-scale self-supervised pre-training on web-scale compressed video repositories. However, the authors do not discuss potential biases inherited from uncurated video data, nor the environmental impact of large-scale pre-training (even if more efficient than optical-flow–based methods). It would be beneficial to include a brief discussion on limitations (e.g., degraded performance on highly compressed or low-frame-rate videos) and on how this approach might be applied responsibly, avoiding misuse in surveillance or privacy-invasive applications.

Conclusion and Recommendation  
Overall, this paper addresses an important practical bottleneck in video self-supervised learning—large storage and computation costs—by capitalizing on motion vectors already present in compressed streams and designing a novel cross-guidance contrastive mechanism. While certain modeling choices (e.g., lack of temporal pretext tasks) could be further enhanced, the empirical gains, clear presentation, and significant efficiency improvements make this a valuable contribution. I therefore recommend acceptance.  

Rating: 6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: The authors propose a self-supervised learning (SSL) method for learning strong visual representations based on compressed videos. Compared with prior work, which sees videos in RGB only, the proposed method is more efficient and leverages the motion information that's already accessible in compressed videos. The authors propose to use a contrastive learning method to supervise on kind of modality with correspondence of another modality. On multiple datasets (mainly UCF and HMDB), good performance is achieved, with both linear probing and transfer learning settings.
reviews.main_review:
[originality: medium-high]
 - The idea of using compressed videos for self-supervised learning is novel/original. Most prior work that operates on compressed videos focues on supervised learning setting only. The implementation of this idea is standard/expected (this is not a drawback).

[quality: medium-high]
 - The paper covers useful ablation study that I'd expect. In particular, the authors demonstrate the usefulness of the compressed-video modality (MV) compared to RGB only (Table 3). Compared to prior work, the performance is also strong while being significantly more efficient. The ablation study that compares with optical flow and low-res optical flow is also interesting (Table 2).

[clarity: high]
 - I think this paper is overall clearly writen. One question about implementation details: How is the similarity calculated in the hard-positive mining procedure?

[significance: medium-high]
 - The area of SSL draws a lot of attention in our community and is of great potential. I think this work extends our knowledge from a new dimension with the use of compressed videos. I think this work can lead to promising new directions in SSL research.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact:
1. In the paper, the authors perform experiments/evaluation solely on videos. I was wondering if the method learns good representations for "image" tasks as well. Namely, leveraging MV can potentially give us better RGB representations for images, too. I wonder what the authors think about this.
2. If the hard-positive mining is based on the learned features, would there be any "chichen-and-egg" problem introduced by the proposed procedure?
3. I like the comparison to MV_{CoViAR}, but I wonder how each specific compression setting affects the results. For example, how does GOP size, the use of B-frame vs P-frame, etc. affect results.

Regarding societal impact, I don't see anything particularly concerning (or more concerning) than other SSL work.
