reviews.text:
Review for “Distributed Learning with Strategic Users: A Repeated Game Approach”

Summary  
This paper studies a federated (or distributed) learning framework in which a fusion center (FC) aggregates gradient updates from strategic, privacy‐conscious users. At each round, each user computes a stochastic gradient on a local mini‐batch, and then strategically chooses whether to send that true (noisy) gradient or to defect by reporting an arbitrary random signal. Since the FC cannot directly verify the authenticity of reported gradients, the authors model the interaction as a repeated 2×2 game and introduce an adaptive zero‐determinant (ZD) incentive mechanism. The FC jointly performs gradient estimation (Eq. 6) and user action classification (Eq. 7), computes detection and false alarm probabilities (Eq. 8), and then adjusts her cooperation probabilities πt via the adaptive ZD strategy (Eq. 9). Theoretical analysis (Lemmas 1–2, Theorems 1–2) establishes that, under mild assumptions on the state transition matrix Ω and estimation error bounds, the stochastic gradient descent with strategic users (SGD‐SU, Algorithm 1) converges at the usual O(1/T) rate up to a controllable bias term. Empirical results on KDD‐Cup ’04 (logistic regression) and MNIST (neural network) confirm that the FC can steer a variety of static user strategies—including tit-for-tat, Pavlov, stubborn, and coin-toss—toward high cooperation rates and effective learning.

Strengths  
- Novelty of approach.  By integrating repeated‐game theory—specifically zero‐determinant strategies—into federated learning, the paper opens a new perspective on incentive design for strategic agents. Extending classical ZD strategies (Press & Dyson [31]) to handle time‐varying stochastic errors (Eq. 12) is nontrivial and fills a gap in recent works on game‐theoretic machine learning.  
- Rigorous analysis.  The bias‐variance decomposition in Eq. (14), combined with the perturbation analysis of the Markov chain (Lemma 1), leads to explicit expressions for the estimation noise (Lemma 2) and bias (Theorem 2). The convergence guarantee in Theorem 1 carefully quantifies how the maximum perturbation HT (Assumption 2) enters the rate.  
- Clear exposition.  The paper is generally well organized: Algorithm 1 succinctly summarizes the overall procedure, and the progression from problem formulation (Sec. 2) to ZD strategies (Sec. 2, Eq. 4) and then to SGD‐SU (Sec. 3) flows naturally. Figures 1 and 2 effectively illustrate the game dynamics and adaptive strategies.  
- Empirical validation.  The experiments use realistic datasets and a variety of user strategies. Figures 4a–4d demonstrate that SGD‐SU achieves small optimality gaps even when users follow noncooperative patterns (e.g., Pavlov), and Figures 4e–4h show robust MNIST performance. The paper also discusses how the FC can estimate Σt and Ξt online (Eq. 23).

Weaknesses and Suggestions  
1. Placement and breadth of related work.  The current manuscript relegates most of the related literature to Appendix A. Given the interdisciplinary nature—spanning federated learning, robust statistics, and game‐theoretic incentives—it would strengthen the paper to move a concise yet substantive discussion into the main text (e.g., end of Sec. 1). In particular, the authors should differentiate their repeated‐game framework from:  
   • Federated learning incentive studies based on contract theory or auctions (e.g., Richardson et al. [32], Liu & Wei [29], Kong et al. [20]).  
   • Robust aggregation methods against Byzantine clients (e.g., Blanchard et al. [2], Su & Xu [33]).  
   • Truth‐elicitation mechanisms in statistical estimation (e.g., Cai et al. [4], Caragiannis et al. [5]).  
   Clarifying how the adaptive ZD strategy differs in assumptions (no penalty, only positive rewards) and goals (ensuring convergence rather than just robustness) will help readers immediately see the novelty.

2. Experimental evaluation with adaptive user strategies.  All reported experiments assume that each user’s strategy vector q is fixed in advance (e.g., stubborn, tit-for-tat). While this is a useful first step, in practice one might expect users to adapt their probabilities online in response to observed rewards. It would be valuable to include at least one experiment where users update their qt over time (for example, best‐response dynamics or reinforcement learning) to verify that the FC’s adaptive ZD strategy indeed drives the long‐term cooperation. Such an experiment could use a simple rule—e.g., users gradually increase q1 if they received reward in the last round—and track whether (Λt q)t converges to near 1. This would test the resilience of the mechanism against adaptive adversaries.

3. Assumptions on observability and stationarity.  The analysis (Lemma 1, Theorem 2) requires that the unperturbed transition matrix Ω is diagonalizable (Assumption 4) and that Λt q≥½ (Assumption 2). It may help to elaborate on how realistic these are. For instance, how sensitive is convergence if the initial cooperation rate Λ1 q is slightly below ½? A brief empirical study showing behavior when Λ1 q=0.45 could illustrate robustness.

4. Moving technical details to the Appendix.  Some lengthy derivations—such as the full expression for Vt (Eq. 20) and the proof of Theorem 2—could remain in the Supplement. In the main text, consider streamlining Eq. (20) to the high‐level insight that Vt depends on past estimation errors and the second‐largest eigenvalue |u2|, referencing the Appendix for full form.

Minor Comments  
- In Sec. 3.1, it might help to remind the reader that Σt = Var(Wk,t) + ν_t^2 I and Ξt denotes the variance of the random signal, to connect the classifier (Eq. 7) more directly to privacy‐preserving noise.  
- On p. 6, after Eq. 12, a few words on why the additive disturbance vanishes when Φt=1 and Ψt=0 would reinforce the intuition.  
- In the experimental description, include the exact hyperparameter settings for the learning rate schedule ηt used in Figures 4e–4h, as well as the GPU type and total training time.

Decision: 6 – Marginally above the acceptance threshold  
Overall, this paper makes a meaningful contribution by bridging repeated‐game strategies with distributed learning convergence. With the suggested expansions—particularly in related work discussion and an adaptive‐user experiment—the paper would be even stronger. I recommend acceptance at a marginally above‐threshold level.

reviews.summary: The paper formulates the interaction between the fusion center (FC) and users in distributed learning (or federated learning) from the perspective of game theory. In order to encourage the users to provide effective information to the FC for model update, the FC adopt zero-determinant (ZD) strategy, associated with which there are some practical issues addressed by the authors. Theoretical analysis on the convergence of the proposed algorithm is presented.
reviews.main_review:
The paper formulates the interaction between the fusion center (FC) and users in distributed learning (or federated learning) from the perspective of game theory. In order to encourage the users to provide effective information to the FC for model update, the FC adopt zero-determinant (ZD) strategy, associated with which there are some practical issues addressed by the authors. Theoretical analysis on the convergence of the proposed algorithm is presented. The formulation and the results are interesting; however, there are some concerns from the reviewer:

1.	What’s the motivation of the users submitting random noninformative signal to the FC instead of just submitting nothing when they decide not to cooperate. Submitting random noninformative signal does not benefit the users but harms the FC. 
2.	Please explain the state transition matrix $\Omega$ in eq. (3), why it is constructed as it is and why the states are supposed to evolve according to it. Just below eq. (3), the authors use \bar{\Omega} to denote the transition matrix, what is the difference between $\Omega$ and $\bar{\Omega}$, or it is just a typo?
3.	The authors are expected to clearly state the goal of users in playing the game, namely, in each iteration, the users maximize what, the expected long-term reward, or the reward of this iteration. Based on the objective of the users, the author shall show that the users’ best response is q* = [1, 1, 1, 1]^T given that the FC adopts ZD strategy. 
4.	In the Experiments section, the figure referred is Fig 2 rather than Fig 4. SGSU is SGD-SU, typo? Does Pavlov mean the win-stay-lose-switch? If it does, please just use one of these two phrases throughout the paper. 
5.	From Figure 2(a) and (b), it is shown that the optimality gap is the smallest when the users take tit-for-tat strategy. Does this mean the FC prefers tit-for-tat instead of full-cooperation in order to obtain a favorable solution to optimization problem (1)? This is not consistent with the game, where the FC prefers full-cooperation? 
6.	Since the problem is formulated as a repeated game, the experiments shall show whether the strategy of the users converges exactly to q* starting from any initial state and action. 
7.	The reviewer believes (24) admits a closed-form solution, and the computational complexity of (24) is the same as that of (6). Please check this.

reviews.summary: This paper proposed an incentive mechanism for a distributed learning setting. The users are encouraged but not obliged to provide the gradient. The results on convergence and an upper bound on the estimation bias of the gradient were shown.
reviews.main_review:
I am mostly concerned about the setup (line 64-line 74). 

“In iteration t, when a user cooperates, he provides an information gain R to the FC at his privacy cost VUR with 0”. It is unclear to me how the authors quantifies the privacy cost and why it is related to the information gain R via a factor V_U between 0 and 1. It seems to me that the information gain R was not used in Algorithm 1. Would the authors explain how R is used? Moreover, the information gain R should vary every time. Is it the case in the paper’s setup?

“The FC is cooperative (Ak,t=C) if she makes a payment r to the user at her cost rVFC with 0”. Why is the cost of the FC rV_{FC} related to V_{FC} via a factor V_FC between 0 and 1? Would the authors explain the intuition behind this?

line 72-73: the authors did not specify how each entry in a payoff vector is ordered.

It would be beneficial to discuss when Phi_t/Psi_t>=p1/p2, Phi_t/Psi_t>=p3/p4 are satisfied and if they are always satisfied (line 151). 

I like it that Theorem 2 gives a bound on \zeta_t. But there are still some unclear things. 

how large is $\sum_{i=1}^{t-1} \alpha_i^2$ compared to $\eta^2$? The authors should establish that the bound (22a) would not be vacuous. Recall in line 61 that the norm of the gradient is <= \ell. This bound is vacuous if the upper bound is just close to \ell. The authors may want to compute the expected value of \zeta_t and then check if the bound is vacuous. 

What is $\Delta_{i,t}$?

The current form looks pretty complicated. The authors may want to do some asymptotic analysis and present the asymptotic order of each term so that the presentation of the results can also be simplified.

reviews.summary: This paper studies the incentives in distributed learning, where the users can choose to join the cooperation or not. The paper formalizes this problem as a two-player game between the fusion center and the user, designs a strategy to learn and provides a convergence guarantee. The contributions include: 1. formalizing of a reward mechanism between FC and users; 2. proposing a strategy for the FC to make users join the cooperation, where FC cannot observe users' actions; 3. providing theoretical guarantee on the convergence rate.
reviews.main_review:
Originality: The task is new. The problem formalization and the mechanism proposed are novel.

Quality: The submission is technically sound. But the assumptions are somewhat unreasonable to me and I would like to know if these assumptions are necessary and non-vacuous. 

Specifically, for Assumption 1, it might be uncommon to make assumptions over the gradient estimate, which is a r.v. dependent on the objective function and the noise distributions. I am curious if the assumption could be made directly over the objective function and the distributions instead of the gradient estimate. For Assumption 2, $\tilde{\Lambda}_t$ depends on $\Lambda_t$, the objective function and the noise distributions. Similar to Assumption 1, it looks quite strange to make direct assumptions over $\tilde{\Lambda}_t$.  Also, before Assumption 2, the authors state that ``" $\Lambda_t q^\top$ and $\tilde{\Lambda}_t q^\top$ must meet the following criteria ...". Is Assumption 2 a necessary condition? 

Besides, the gradient estimator $\hat m_t$ depends on $q$. It is unclear to me what $q$ is.  Is it the user's initial strategy? Is it deterministic?

Clarity: Some notations are unclear. It would be better if the paper has more explanation on the assumptions.

Significance: The problem studied by this paper is interesting and important.

Other comments:
1. What is $Q$ in Eq. (8)?
2. Is $\hat {m}_t^\top (m_t - \hat m_t)$ in Assumption 1 always positive? If not, $\sqrt{\hat m_t^\top (m_t - \hat m_t)}$  is not well-defined.
3. Typo: $Ray(\hat m_t, \Sigma_t)$ should be $Ray(\Sigma_t,\hat m_t)$.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: No. The authors are recommended to add a discussion on the societal impact.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper studies the incentives in distributed learning, where the users can choose to join the cooperation or not. The paper formalizes this problem as a two-player game between the fusion center and the user, designs a strategy to learn and provides a convergence guarantee. The contributions include: 1. formalizing of a reward mechanism between FC and users; 2. proposing a strategy for the FC to make users join the cooperation, where FC cannot observe users' actions; 3. providing theoretical guarantee on the convergence rate.

Main Review:
Originality: The task is new. The problem formalization and the mechanism proposed are novel.

Quality: The submission is technically sound. But the assumptions are somewhat unreasonable to me and I would like to know if these assumptions are necessary and non-vacuous. 

Specifically, for Assumption 1, it might be uncommon to make assumptions over the gradient estimate, which is a r.v. dependent on the objective function and the noise distributions. I am curious if the assumption could be made directly over the objective function and the distributions instead of the gradient estimate. For Assumption 2, $\tilde{\Lambda}_t$ depends on $\Lambda_t$, the objective function and the noise distributions. Similar to Assumption 1, it looks quite strange to make direct assumptions over $\tilde{\Lambda}_t$.  Also, before Assumption 2, the authors state that ``" $\Lambda_t q^\top$ and $\tilde{\Lambda}_t q^\top$ must meet the following criteria ...". Is Assumption 2 a necessary condition? 

Besides, the gradient estimator $\hat m_t$ depends on $q$. It is unclear to me what $q$ is.  Is it the user's initial strategy? Is it deterministic?

Clarity: Some notations are unclear. It would be better if the paper has more explanation on the assumptions.

Significance: The problem studied by this paper is interesting and important.

Other comments:
1. What is $Q$ in Eq. (8)?
2. Is $\hat {m}_t^\top (m_t - \hat m_t)$ in Assumption 1 always positive? If not, $\sqrt{\hat m_t^\top (m_t - \hat m_t)}$  is not well-defined.
3. Typo: $Ray(\hat m_t, \Sigma_t)$ should be $Ray(\Sigma_t,\hat m_t)$.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
No. The authors are recommended to add a discussion on the societal impact.

Rating:
5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper studies the incentives in a distributed learning setting where privacy-aware, strategic users can choose to cooperate or defect when reporting gradient updates to a fusion center (FC). The interaction is modeled as a two-player repeated game, and the FC cannot directly observe the users’ true actions or validate their gradient reports. To address this, the authors propose a joint gradient estimation and user classification scheme coupled with an adaptive zero-determinant (ZD) reward mechanism for the FC. Theoretical analysis provides convergence guarantees for a Stochastic Gradient Descent algorithm with Strategic Users (SGD-SU), and experiments on the KDD-Cup’04 and MNIST datasets demonstrate the empirical performance under various user strategies.

Originality:
The task—incorporating strategic behavior into distributed learning via repeated game theory—is novel. Formalizing the FC–user interaction as a 2×2 repeated game with private gradient noise and unobservable actions departs from existing federated learning models. The use of ZD strategies, generalized to handle time-varying stochastic errors and coupled with a data-driven classification rule, represents a creative extension of both machine learning and game-theoretic techniques in the federated context.

Technical Quality and Assumptions:
The submission is technically sound: the repeated game formulation in Section 2, the derivation of the Markov transition matrix Ω in Eq. (3), and the extension to adaptive strategies in Eq. (9) are logically consistent. Lemma 1 characterizes how perturbations from classification errors (Eq. (12)) affect the stationary distribution of game states, and Theorems 1 and 2 provide convergence bounds and bias estimates for SGD-SU.

However, some key assumptions appear strong or potentially vacuous:

• Assumption 1 requires a lower bound on ∥ẑmₜ∥ so that the linear classifier of Eq. (7) can reliably separate cooperative from defective gradient reports. Yet ẑmₜ is itself a random estimator dependent on the objective F and noise covariances Σₜ, Ξₜ. It would be more natural to impose conditions directly on F (e.g., gradient magnitudes away from zero) or on the data noise distributions, rather than on the realized ∥ẑmₜ∥ at each step. Are there concrete examples (e.g., logistic regression with bounded features) where Assumption 1 holds non-vacuously?

• Assumption 2 demands that both Λₜ qᵀ and its noisy counterpart ˜Λₜ qᵀ remain above certain positive thresholds (half and zero, respectively). Since ˜Λₜ depends on the history of plays and on the FC’s adaptive strategies πₜ (through classification), it is unclear whether this assumption is necessary for convergence or simply a technical convenience. The paper states before Assumption 2 that “Λₜ qᵀ and ˜Λₜ qᵀ must meet the following criteria…”, but does not clarify whether these conditions are also sufficient or can be relaxed.

• The notation q—which denotes the user’s memory-1 strategy vector q=[q₁,q₂,q₃,q₄]—is not clearly defined when it first appears in Section 2. Is q assumed to be a fixed, deterministic strategy chosen by each user ahead of time? Or is q learned or adapted over time? The gradient estimator in Eq. (6) depends explicitly on q, implying that the FC must know or estimate the user’s q in advance. A more detailed discussion of how q is obtained or estimated in practice would strengthen the technical presentation.

Clarity:
While the overall presentation is coherent, a few areas would benefit from further explanation:

1. In Eq. (8), the symbol Q(·) represents the tail probability of the standard normal distribution, but this is not stated. A brief reminder (“Q(z)=Pr[N(0,1)>z]”) would help readers unfamiliar with detection theory.

2. In Assumption 1, the term √(ẑmₜᵀ(mₜ−ẑmₜ)) appears. If ẑmₜᵀ(mₜ−ẑmₜ) can be negative, the square root is not well-defined. Clarification on why this inner product is assumed nonnegative or how its sign is controlled is needed.

3. In several places (e.g., definition of Ray(Σₜ,ẑmₜ) in the Appendix), the ordering of arguments in the Rayleigh quotient is inconsistent. The authors note a typo (“Ray(ẑmₜ,Σₜ) should be Ray(Σₜ,ẑmₜ)”), but a unified notation review would improve readability.

Significance:
The paper addresses an important challenge: how to design incentive mechanisms for federated learning when participants are strategic and privacy-sensitive. The blend of repeated games and stochastic optimization is conceptually appealing. If the strong assumptions can be grounded in realistic settings, this line of work could have significant impact on robust federated learning and mechanism design for data markets.

Additional Observations and Suggestions:
– The experiments in Section 4 (Figs. 4a–4h) illustrate convergence under various user strategies (stubborn, tit-for-tat, Pavlov, full cooperation) and reward schemes (equalizer vs. extortion ZD). It would strengthen the empirical study to include ablation on the classification threshold (Eq. 7) and on mis-specification of Σₜ, Ξₜ when they are estimated via Eq. (23).

– The authors mention that code and instructions are provided in the supplement. Including a brief description of computing resources (GPU/CPU, runtime) in the main text would help assess the practical viability of SGD-SU.

– A short discussion on potential negative societal impacts (e.g., misuse of reward mechanisms, gaming by malicious users) and on ethical considerations in strategic gradient sharing would address the NeurIPS checklist item on societal impact.

Minor Comments:
1. What is the computational complexity of solving Eq. (9) for πₜ versus directly using p?  
2. In Eq. (23), the definitions of \bar Yᶜₜ and \bar Yᵈₜ appear swapped in the sums (both sum over Kᶜₜ).  
3. The notation “Submitted to NeurIPS 2020. Do not distribute.” in the header can be removed in future camera-ready versions.

Decision:
5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold. While technically sound and novel in its integration of repeated game incentives with distributed SGD, the paper relies on strong assumptions that are not fully justified or illustrated in realistic scenarios. Clarifying or relaxing Assumptions 1–2 and providing additional context on the user strategy q would be necessary to bring this work to readiness for publication.

title: Reinforcement learning for instance segmentation with high-level priors

reviews.summary: This paper presents a strategy for object instance segmentation from images using training data that has no manual annotations. Instead of manual annotations, as is required for many state-of-the-art methods, this paper proposes to use high-level priors represented as rules that can be evaluated in the produced segmentation. These rules define what an acceptable segmentation is, and are used to create a reward signal for a reinforcement learning agent. The framework formulates an environment, an agent, an action space and a reward system in a stateless system to guide segmentation. All the components of the system are well designed and well thought, and this approach may have wide applications in bioimage segmentation problems.
reviews.main_review:
* The stateless actor-critic model has connections with the dynamics of Generative Adversarial Networks, as the authors note. The fact that there is no state also begs the question of what are the connections with the REINFORCE rule for training models when the loss function is non-differentiable, and how that approach could simplify or enhance the proposed formulation.
* The experimental design is interesting and overall complete. However, the performance metrics used for evaluation are not in line with research in the instance segmentation field, where precision and recall are used in several different ways. For example, computing precision or F1 score at specific intersection over union thresholds would be more informative to understand how many objects / regions are correctly segmented and to what level of overlap with ground truth.
* There are a few datasets studied in the instance segmentation literature that could have been used as benchmarks in this evaluation. At least one dataset used for nucleus segmentation of cells, for instance, would add a lot of value to the paper for two reasons: first, it connects with previous literature in the topic, second, it allows to assess how far a model trained without ground truth data would go for segmenting these objects. It is not expected that the proposed model should be better than fully supervised approaches, but being the first in the unsupervised regime would help understand how far we are from a competitive model while not requiring ground truth data.
* The paper is overall well written and clear.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: No comments about limitations. Positive societal impacts expected, as less annotations would be required from experts to obtain accurate models.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper presents a strategy for object instance segmentation from images using training data that has no manual annotations. Instead of manual annotations, as is required for many state-of-the-art methods, this paper proposes to use high-level priors represented as rules that can be evaluated in the produced segmentation. These rules define what an acceptable segmentation is, and are used to create a reward signal for a reinforcement learning agent. The framework formulates an environment, an agent, an action space and a reward system in a stateless system to guide segmentation. All the components of the system are well designed and well thought, and this approach may have wide applications in bioimage segmentation problems.

Main Review:
* The stateless actor-critic model has connections with the dynamics of Generative Adversarial Networks, as the authors note. The fact that there is no state also begs the question of what are the connections with the REINFORCE rule for training models when the loss function is non-differentiable, and how that approach could simplify or enhance the proposed formulation.
* The experimental design is interesting and overall complete. However, the performance metrics used for evaluation are not in line with research in the instance segmentation field, where precision and recall are used in several different ways. For example, computing precision or F1 score at specific intersection over union thresholds would be more informative to understand how many objects / regions are correctly segmented and to what level of overlap with ground truth.
* There are a few datasets studied in the instance segmentation literature that could have been used as benchmarks in this evaluation. At least one dataset used for nucleus segmentation of cells, for instance, would add a lot of value to the paper for two reasons: first, it connects with previous literature in the topic, second, it allows to assess how far a model trained without ground truth data would go for segmenting these objects. It is not expected that the proposed model should be better than fully supervised approaches, but being the first in the unsupervised regime would help understand how far we are from a competitive model while not requiring ground truth data.
* The paper is overall well written and clear.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
No comments about limitations. Positive societal impacts expected, as less annotations would be required from experts to obtain accurate models.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper presents a novel strategy for object instance segmentation that operates without any manually annotated training data. Instead of relying on costly pixel-level labels, the authors propose harnessing high-level priors—encoded as non-differentiable rules or expectations about object shape, count, and arrangement—to guide a reinforcement learning (RL)–based segmentation pipeline. In particular, instance segmentation is formulated as a graph partitioning problem over image superpixels, and a stateless actor-critic agent predicts edge weights in the region adjacency graph. The non-differentiable reward signals are derived from evaluating the conformity of the resulting segmentation to the specified rules. This framework is one of the first practical applications of a stateless actor-critic setup in vision, and the experiments on both synthetic “circles on structured background” data and a real developmental biology microscopy dataset demonstrate that the method achieves competitive results using only high-level priors. All components—state formulation, action space, and reward decomposition—are carefully designed, and the approach shows promise for bioimage analysis where expert annotations are scarce.

Main Review:

1. Connection to existing RL and gradient estimation methods
   * The authors ground their approach in stateless actor-critic RL (Section 3.1) and correctly note its theoretical parallels to adversarial training (Pfau & Vinyals, 2016). Because the entire segmentation pipeline includes non-differentiable steps (graph partitioning via multicut and rule-based reward computations), this choice is well motivated.
   * It would strengthen the paper to more explicitly discuss the connection to classic policy gradient methods, such as REINFORCE (Williams, 1992), which also handle non-differentiable reward functions by estimating gradients through sampling. The authors might compare how their stateless actor-critic gradient estimator (Equation 2) differs in sample complexity or stability from a straightforward REINFORCE baseline applied to the same graph-partitioning action space.
   * Additional ablations could explore whether omitting the critic entirely—i.e., using pure policy gradient with a global reward—would degrade performance in practice. Given the high dimensionality of the action (all edge weights predicted in one shot), such an experiment would emphasize the added value of the critic in reducing variance.

2. Evaluation metrics and reporting
   * In the microscopy experiment (Section 4.2, Table 1), the authors report Symmetric Best Dice (SBD) and the two components of Variation of Information (VI split and VI merge). These are established measures in instance segmentation literature, especially in biomedical settings [Meilă, 2003; Rand, 1971].
   * To facilitate a broader comparison with the computer vision community, the authors should additionally report precision, recall, and F1-score at different Intersection over Union (IoU) thresholds (e.g., IoU = 0.5, 0.75). Such metrics are standard in benchmarks like COCO and the 2018 Data Science Bowl (nucleus segmentation). This change would more directly quantify how many predicted objects match ground truth at varying overlap criteria and allow readers to compare against a wider range of methods.
   * Furthermore, reporting metrics such as the continuous Dice coefficient [Shamir et al., 2019] might provide further insight into how the soft edge weights before graph partitioning correlate with final segmentation quality.

3. Dataset choice and benchmarking
   * The synthetic “circles” dataset (Section 4.1, Figures 3–4) nicely illustrates that the method can recover object shapes based solely on circularity priors and global count estimates. The structured background and self-supervised contrastive pretraining for superpixel node features highlight the flexibility of the framework.
   * For the real-data evaluation (Section 4.2, Figure 5), the authors focus on a light-sheet microscopy dataset of developing Drosophila embryonic cells, using boundary predictions from a pre-trained plant-tissue network [Wolny et al., 2020] to generate superpixels. This is an excellent use case, but to further contextualize results it would be valuable to include at least one publicly available nucleus or cell segmentation dataset, such as the MoNuSeg or Data Science Bowl nucleus challenge. Even if only a subset of rules (e.g., shape, size) can be applied, this benchmark would help quantify the gap between unsupervised, rule-driven training and fully supervised approaches on widely studied data.
   * The authors rightly emphasize that new priors can be plugged into their RL pipeline without handcrafted differentiable relaxations. Running experiments on a standard nucleus dataset—where shape priors (e.g., ellipticity, convexity) are well understood—would underline the generality of the approach and facilitate comparisons with methods like Cellpose [Stringer et al., 2021] or star-convex segmentation [Schmidt et al., 2018].

4. Design of reward decomposition
   * The localized supervision via sub-graph–level rewards (Section 3.2, Algorithm 1, Figure 2) is a particularly strong point. By decomposing a global object-level reward into overlapping subgraphs, the authors mitigate the credit-assignment problem in high-dimensional action spaces.
   * It would be instructive to include an ablation in which rewards are either (i) assigned only at the global level or (ii) directly at the object level (without sub-graph extraction). The paper mentions that a direct object-level actor-critic setup yielded inferior results (Appendix), but showing quantitative differences in a plot or table would clarify how much localized reward decomposition contributes to faster convergence and better segmentation.
   * Moreover, discussing trade-offs in the choice of subgraph sizes (e.g., 6, 12, 32, 128 edges) and how they relate to object size in pixels would help practitioners set these parameters for new datasets.

5. Clarity and writing
   * The manuscript is generally well written, with clear descriptions of the state, action, and reward components (Sections 2–3). The figures—particularly Figure 1 (agent-environment interaction) and Figures 4–5 (qualitative results)—effectively illustrate each part of the pipeline.
   * A small suggestion is to include pseudo-code for the overall training loop, integrating the actor update, critic update, and reward decomposition steps, to help readers implement the method more easily.
   * In Section 4.2, abbreviations such as “SBD” and “VI” are introduced without immediate definitions in the main text; adding brief explanations or referring the reader to the Appendix/table captions would improve readability.

Limitations and Societal Impact:
The paper does not explicitly discuss limitations beyond noting that the method depends on reasonably informative priors and meaningful superpixel features. It would strengthen the discussion to mention potential failure modes—for example, when priors conflict or are too weak to guide learning, or when spurious background textures lead to poor graph over-segmentations. Regarding societal impact, the approach has clear positive implications by reducing expert annotation burden in biomedical research. Since no human subjects data are used and no safety-critical deployment is envisioned, negative impacts appear minimal.

Overall Recommendation: 7 (Good paper, accept)
This manuscript introduces a compelling framework for instance segmentation driven entirely by high-level priors and reinforcement learning. The stateless actor-critic formulation, the reward decomposition via subgraphs, and the demonstration on synthetic and real microscopy data contribute solid advances in unsupervised and weakly supervised segmentation. Addressing the above points regarding evaluation metrics, benchmarking on standard datasets, and deeper analysis of reward design would further strengthen the paper, but these are suggestions for improvement rather than reasons for rejection. The paper is well suited for acceptance.

reviews.summary: This paper presents a RL approach for instance segmentation which formulates the instance segmentation problem as graph partitioning and the actor critic predicts the edge weights driven by the rewards, which are based on the conformity of segmented instances to high-level priors on object shape, position and size.
reviews.main_review:
This paper presents a novel idea of applying RL methodology to instance segmentation. The stateless actor-critic reinforcement learning theme enables non-differential rewards which could incorporate prior knowledge to guide the segmentation, and relaxes the heavy requirement of pixel-wise labeling. The idea is unique and quite interesting.
The related work looks sufficient to me. The experiments are well designed with convincing results.
The writing is overall clear, but there are a few parts which could be improved:
1. For Section3, it's not clear to me the essential difference between Method 1 & 2. It'd be nice to include a graph as Figure 1 to demonstrate Method 2, instead of only including it in Appendix.
2. There is a lot of valuable information in Appendix. It'd be nice to figure out a way to shorten some parts of Method section (such as Section 3.2 and experiments) and include some content from Appendix here.
3. For the experiments, some failure cases and analysis are necessary. It'd be good to add a section of ablation studies.

reviews.text:
Review for “Stateless actor-critic for instance segmentation with high-level priors”

Summary  
This paper introduces an end-to-end instance segmentation framework that leverages reinforcement learning (RL) in lieu of a fully differentiable loss.  The authors recast instance segmentation as a graph partitioning problem over superpixels, where a stateless actor-critic RL agent predicts edge weights and a non-differentiable graph-cut step extracts instances.  Reward signals are crafted from high-level, non-differentiable priors on object shape (e.g., circularity via Circle Hough Transform), position or size, and decomposed over localized subgraphs for stable training.  Experiments on both synthetic “circles on structured background” data and a challenging microscopy dataset of Drosophila embryonic cells demonstrate that, with no pixel-wise ground-truth annotations and only rule-based supervision, the method achieves plausible segmentations that substantially outperform region-growing and embedding-based baselines.

Main Review

Strengths  
1. Novel RL formulation for instance segmentation.  To my knowledge, this is one of the first practical applications of a *stateless* actor-critic paradigm in segmentation.  By treating the graph partitioning step as part of the environment, the approach neatly sidesteps the need to make high-level priors differentiable.  This generalizes beyond shape constraints to any rule that can be evaluated on final instances.  
2. Reward decomposition strategy.  The paper proposes a clear algorithm for extracting overlapping subgraphs of fixed size (e.g. 6, 12, 32 edges) and mapping image- and object-level rewards onto them.  This localized supervision balances between global coherence and granular feedback.  
3. Compelling experiments.  On the synthetic circles dataset, the method achieves clean disk separation despite structured textured background, whereas mutex-watershed alone fails.  On real light-sheet microscopy images, the system learns from priors on radial cell arrangement and approximate bounding-box aspect ratios to segment hundreds of cells without any masks.  Quantitative VI and Symmetric Best Dice scores (Table 1) confirm the method outperforms baselines that rely on pretrained edge detectors or contrastive embeddings.  
4. End-to-end training.  In “Method 1,” the U-Net feature extractor is integrated into the actor and is fine-tuned by policy gradients, leading to learned features that transition from raw microscopy intensities to a semantically meaningful boundary representation (Fig. 5).  

Weaknesses and Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Clarify Method 1 vs. Method 2 (Section 3).  
   – The manuscript introduces two variants: (i) **Method 1**, where the U-Net feature extractor is *inside* the agent and trained jointly; and (ii) **Method 2**, where node features are *fixed* after pretraining.  However, the essential differences in data flow, training schedule, and use cases are not crisply contrasted in the main text.  
   – Suggestion: Add a schematic analogous to Fig. 1 for Method 2 in the main body (currently only in the Appendix).  Label inputs, outputs and gradients clearly for both methods side by side.  A small table summarizing trade-offs (e.g., ease of pretraining vs. fully unsupervised capability) would help readers choose between them.  
2. Overlong Method and Experiment sections; heavy reliance on Appendix.  
   – Section 3.2 (Localized Supervision Signals) and parts of Section 4 carry detailed algorithmic steps (e.g., mapping object rewards to edges, subgraph extraction pseudocode) that, while valuable, break the narrative flow.  
   – Suggestion: Move some of the lower-level derivations (e.g., precise formulas Equations 5–8, Algorithm 1 pseudocode) to a “Methods Appendix” and summarize the key ideas in the main text.  For instance, bullet the subgraph requirements (connectedness, fixed size, coverage, overlap) and illustrate with the small toy graph in Fig. 2, then refer to the Appendix for implementation details.  
3. Lack of failure case analysis and ablation studies.  
   – While the presented results are promising, the paper does not discuss scenarios where the method struggles: e.g., what happens if the subgraph size is chosen poorly, or if rewards are too noisy?  Does the training ever collapse to trivial solutions (all-merge or all-split)?  
   – Suggestion: Include a short subsection analyzing typical failure modes.  For example, on the circles dataset, show a case where noisy self-supervised features mislead the actor into over-grouping disks; discuss how reward gradients guide it back.  Similarly, an ablation over key hyperparameters—subgraph sizes, number of reward types, temperature α in Equation 2—would quantify their impact on stability and performance.  
4. Comparison to additional baselines.  
   – The paper compares against mutex-watershed and discriminative embedding clustering, which are reasonable choices.  However, recent weakly supervised segmentation losses (e.g., constraining the Jaccard index via soft surrogates [14]) could serve as additional baselines.  
   – Suggestion: If feasible, add a small-scale experiment in which one of the simpler differentiable priors from [6] is used directly in a U-Net loss, to show that RL can handle richer, non-differentiable rules that such surrogate losses cannot encode.  
5. Minor clarity issues.  
   – In Equation 2, clarify whether the joint action probability is over continuous Gaussians or after the sigmoid.  A brief note on numerical stability of sampling vs. deterministic inference at test time would help.  
   – In Section 4.2, define all variables in Equations 9–11 in the main text (γ, η, δ, a).  A small splash-out of the Gaussian weighting scheme in Figure 5 would make the reward design more tangible.

Conclusion and Recommendation  
Overall, this work presents a novel and well-motivated framework for integrating high-level, non-differentiable priors into instance segmentation via a stateless actor-critic RL setup.  The contributions—including the reward decomposition over subgraphs and the end-to-end training of graph-based agents—represent a significant step beyond existing differentiable surrogate losses.  While the paper would benefit from improved clarity around the two method variants, consolidation of some technical details, and additional ablations or failure analyses, these are largely organizational concerns.  The core idea is unique, the system is implemented carefully, and the experiments convincingly demonstrate its utility in challenging low-data regimes.  

I therefore recommend acceptance (Rating: **6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold**).
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper presents a RL approach for instance segmentation which formulates the instance segmentation problem as graph partitioning and the actor critic predicts the edge weights driven by the rewards, which are based on the conformity of segmented instances to high-level priors on object shape, position and size.

Main Review:
This paper presents a novel idea of applying RL methodology to instance segmentation. The stateless actor-critic reinforcement learning theme enables non-differential rewards which could incorporate prior knowledge to guide the segmentation, and relaxes the heavy requirement of pixel-wise labeling. The idea is unique and quite interesting.
The related work looks sufficient to me. The experiments are well designed with convincing results.
The writing is overall clear, but there are a few parts which could be improved:
1. For Section3, it's not clear to me the essential difference between Method 1 & 2. It'd be nice to include a graph as Figure 1 to demonstrate Method 2, instead of only including it in Appendix.
2. There is a lot of valuable information in Appendix. It'd be nice to figure out a way to shorten some parts of Method section (such as Section 3.2 and experiments) and include some content from Appendix here.
3. For the experiments, some failure cases and analysis are necessary. It'd be good to add a section of ablation studies.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: In this paper, the authors proposed a reinforcement learning (RL) framework for instance segmentation with superpixels. The proposed framework is based on a stateless actor-critic setup and is demonstrated to be effective on the instance segmentation tasks for one toy dataset and one microscopy dataset.
reviews.main_review:
+: strength
-: weakness 

1)Originality: 

+: This paper proposes to solve the instance segmentation tasks with weak supervision (superpixels) using the RL based methods. Different from the typical weakly supervised image instance segmentation methods, the RL-based one is a new and interesting application.

-: The authors only talked about the related work on RL however ignore the related work on instance segmentation (fully supervised and weakly supervised). 

2) Quality:

+: The proposed modules are proved to be effective via the ablation studies on the microscopy dataset. In addition, the authors also provide sufficient analysis on the selection of the hyper-parameters, such as the number of feature channels, random seed, etc.

-: There lack extensive experiments on public instance segmentation benchmark datasets, such as MS COCO [a]. In addition, there are also many instance segmentation tasks in microscopy image analysis, such as nuclei instance segmentation in histopathology images [b], cell instance segmentation in electron microscopy images [1].

[a] https://cocodataset.org/#home
[b] A Multi-Organ Nucleus Segmentation Challenge, in IEEE TMI
[1]: the reference #1 in the manuscript.

3) Clarity:

+: The methods and experiment sections are clearly presented.

-: This paper aims at solving the weakly supervised instance segmentation via RL architecture, however, there lack illustrations and discussion on any related instance segmentation works.

4) Significance:

-: Although the authors have presented many experimental results, there still lack experimental results on representative benchmark instance segmentation datasets on general and medical images, which makes me feel that the overall paper lacks significance.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact:
1) The major concern of this work is the lack of experimental results on public benchmark datasets. For the first toy dataset, the background content seems to be fixed (black content and white ‘noise’). For the second microscopy dataset, the morphological structure of the instances is always special: all the instances overlapping together to form a circle-like fruitfly embryo. To this end, the overall experiments on the two datasets are not sufficient to demonstrate the proposed method can be effective on other general datasets, such as the MS COCO. This made the overall impact of the proposed method questionable.  

2) It seems that there are only 10 testing images in the real microscopy dataset, which is a small number. Therefore, a statistical significance test (based on p-value) is suggested for the results on this dataset.

3) Computational complexity analysis is also suggested on the proposed framework.

Main Review:

-: weakness

-: The authors only talked about the related work on RL however ignore the related work on instance segmentation (fully supervised and weakly supervised).

-: There lack extensive experiments on public instance segmentation benchmark datasets, such as MS COCO [a]. In addition, there are also many instance segmentation tasks in microscopy image analysis, such as nuclei instance segmentation in histopathology images [b], cell instance segmentation in electron microscopy images [1].

[a] https://cocodataset.org/#home
[b] A Multi-Organ Nucleus Segmentation Challenge, in IEEE TMI
[1]: the reference #1 in the manuscript.

3) Clarity:

+: The methods and experiment sections are clearly presented.

-: This paper aims at solving the weakly supervised instance segmentation via RL architecture, however, there lack illustrations and discussion on any related instance segmentation works.

4) Significance:

Limitations And Societal Impact:

For the first toy dataset, the background content seems to be fixed (black content and white ‘noise’). reviews.text:
Summary:
The manuscript “Stateless actor-critic for instance segmentation with high-level priors” introduces an end-to-end instance segmentation framework based on a stateless actor-critic reinforcement learning (RL) paradigm. Instead of relying on differentiable losses or full pixel-level supervision, the method formulates instance segmentation as a graph partitioning problem over superpixels. A graph neural network (GNN) actor predicts edge weights which are then partitioned via a non-differentiable multicut solver; a critic network learns to approximate rewards derived from high-level non-differentiable priors (e.g., shape circularity, spatial arrangement). The authors demonstrate the approach on a synthetic “Circles” dataset and on a light-sheet microscopy dataset of fruit‐fly embryos, showing that their RL setup can achieve reasonable segmentation quality with only rule-based supervision.

Originality:
+ The use of a stateless actor-critic setup for instance segmentation is novel in the segmentation literature. While reinforcement learning has been applied to sequential mask proposals [8, 9], this work predicts all edge weights in a single step, a fresh and interesting twist that simplifies the pipeline and allows learning from non-differentiable priors.  
- However, the related work discussion is narrowly focused on RL methods and far less on the broader family of instance segmentation approaches—both fully supervised schemes (e.g. Mask R-CNN [34], proposal-based networks) and other weakly supervised or prior-driven methods (e.g. shape priors via CRFs, adversarial losses, semantic embedding losses). The omission of key segmentation works such as bounding-box based weak supervision [b], discriminative embedding methods [28, 48], and topology-aware losses [20] leaves the reader unclear on how the proposed RL framework compares to these established lines of work. Expanding the literature review to include these areas would strengthen the paper’s contextualization and highlight the specific gap that the stateless RL setup fills.

Quality:
+ The authors perform a careful ablation study on the microscopy dataset: Table 1 compares variants with and without handcrafted features, with pre-trained contrastive embeddings, and with or without the additional edge map channel. The impact of learned vs. fixed features, augmentation noise, and the role of background vs. foreground priors is quantitatively assessed using Symmetric Best Dice (SBD) and Variation of Information (VI). The hyperparameters (such as number of feature channels, subgraph sizes of 6, 12, 32, 128 edges, learning rates, and temperature α) are selected via cross-validation and stability over different random seeds is documented in the supplementary.  
- A key limitation is the lack of evaluation on widely used public benchmarks. The synthetic toy dataset (“Circles”) uses a fixed black-and-white background with Gaussian noise and has only circular disks, which is far from natural scene complexity. The fruitfly embryo dataset comprises only 317 images, with merely 10 held-out ground truth labels—insufficient to claim generalizability or statistical robustness. There are established public microscopy instance segmentation challenges (e.g. MoNuSeg, BBBC datasets) and general-purpose benchmarks like MS COCO [a] that should be used to demonstrate broader applicability. Without such experiments, it is difficult to assess whether the framework will scale to diverse instance shapes, varying textures, or cluttered scenes. A statistical significance test (e.g. p-values or confidence intervals across multiple random seeds) is also needed given the small test set size.

Clarity:
+ The method description is clear: Section 3.1 explains the stateless actor-critic formulation, the GNN convolutional layers (Equations 1 and 3), and the loss gradients (Equation 2). The reward decomposition into fixed-size subgraphs is illustrated in Figure 2 and formalized in the Appendix, making the approach concrete.  
- On the other hand, the reader would benefit from a more explicit pseudocode or workflow diagram summarizing the training loop—particularly how sub-graphs are sampled (Algorithm 1 in the Appendix) and how object-level priors (e.g. bounding box template matching, Circle Hough Transform) are mapped to subgraph rewards (Equations 5–8). The connection between high-level biological priors on embryo cell orientation and the actual reward functions could be elaborated with additional illustrative figures. Moreover, direct comparisons to architectural diagrams of fully supervised baselines would situate the contribution more transparently.

Significance:
- While the idea of integrating non-differentiable high-level priors via RL is conceptually appealing, the current experiments do not convincingly establish the method’s significance for the broader segmentation community. The synthetic dataset is too simplified, and the microscopy dataset is narrowly specialized (fruitfly embryo cells forming a circular pattern). Without results on challenging, varied datasets—natural images with thousands of object categories or other biomedical modalities like histopathology nuclei segmentation—the work’s impact remains limited. Demonstrating favorable performance or data-efficiency gains on at least one standard benchmark would elevate the practical significance.

Limitations and Suggestions:
1) The fixed “black + white noise” background in the synthetic experiment and the circular embryo cell arrangement in the microscopy data are not representative of typical segmentation scenarios. To validate generalizability, I recommend testing on at least one public dataset with varied object shapes and backgrounds (e.g., MS COCO for natural scenes or MoNuSeg for histopathology).  
2) The real microscopy test set contains only 10 annotated images. The authors should perform statistical significance testing (e.g., paired t-tests, bootstrap confidence intervals) across multiple random seeds to substantiate that the observed improvements are not due to chance.  
3) A computational complexity and runtime analysis is missing. Given that RL training can be expensive, it would be useful to report the total training time (noted as “4 days on an RTX 3090” in Section 4.2) and inference speed per image, and to compare this to standard CNN-based segmenters or graph-cut methods in terms of GPU/CPU usage and memory footprint. This would clarify the practical trade-offs involved.

Overall, the paper introduces an interesting novel framework—stateless actor-critic RL for instance segmentation with non-differentiable priors—but falls short on comprehensive evaluation and contextual positioning within the larger body of segmentation research. Additional experiments on public benchmarks, more extensive significance analyses, and expanded related work discussion would be necessary to raise the work to the level of broad impact.

Rating:
5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: The paper proposes the use of RL for instance segmentation. In particular, the paper uses super pixels together with graph partitioning and RL to learn the labels of instances. The model is validated on two datasets, one synthetic and one biomedical imaging dataset and is numerically compared to two approaches ([28] and [47]).
reviews.main_review:
**Originality:**

In the paper, the main motivation of the use of RL is to enable optimization of discrete objective. However, this use of RL is standard. Moreover, given this paper motivations, the reviewer would expect to see empirical comparisons to alternative methods to learn discrete assignments  (e.g. straight through, DETR-style https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.12872, [8]). Thus, from the methodological point of view the novelty is rather limited. From the application point of view, the instance segmentation of light microscopy images, the paper might have some edge of originality; however, it is hard to assess it given the niche domain. To fully appreciate the originality of the method it would be beneficial to validate the approach on a more standard benchmark. 


**Clarity:**

The paper is generally clearly written. However, the clarity of the methods could be improved. The presentation of this section is a bit convoluted making it hard to grasp the overall picture of the proposed approach. The reviewer would encourage the authors to simplify the presentation of the Methods, especially focusing on the aspects of why particular decisions were made. E.g. why are the authors considering method 1 and 2? Why stateless RL with sequence length of 1? Why not use straight through?

For a non-microscopy expert, it is hard to understand the color codings of Figs 4 and 5. 


**Quality and significance:**

The quality and significance of the results are hard to assess given the niche application domain. It would be beneficial to test the ideas on some more standard benchmarks to instance image segmentation (e.g. COCO). 

Additional questions comments:
- Why are superpixels a good prior for this task? 
- Would it be possible to use other priors/partitioning methods?
- It would be nice to discuss the tradeoffs between added computational costs due to the use of RL and metric gains.
- Would it be possible to report the numbers of a standard instance segmentation model for this task, (e.g. https://arxiv.org/abs/1703.06870 or some followup)?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: There is no limitation section/paragraph nor societal impact section/paragraph. Please add those to the conclusions section.
reviews.rating: 4: Ok but not good enough - rejection

reviews.text:
Review for “Stateless actor-critic for instance segmentation with high-level priors”

Summary:  
This manuscript presents an instance segmentation approach based on a stateless actor-critic reinforcement learning (RL) framework. The key idea is to formulate segmentation as graph partitioning of superpixels: an actor network (a graph neural network, GNN) predicts edge weights for a region adjacency graph, these weights are passed through a non-differentiable multicut solver to obtain final instance labels, and a critic network learns to approximate non-differentiable reward signals derived from high-level priors (e.g., object shape circularity, size, and spatial distribution). The authors demonstrate the approach on (i) a synthetic “circles on structured background” dataset, using Circle Hough Transform rewards, and (ii) a real 2D light-sheet microscopy dataset of developing Drosophila embryos, leveraging shape and radial pattern priors. They report metrics such as Symmetric Best Dice (SBD) and Variation of Information (VI) and compare to baselines including discriminative embedding [28] and boundary-based multicut [47].

Overall and Decision:  
While the proposed formulation is interesting in that it encapsulates non-differentiable rules into an RL framework and avoids handcrafted relaxations of loss functions, the methodological novelty is limited, and the empirical validation is narrowly focused on niche datasets. The paper in its current form does not convincingly demonstrate broad applicability or significant improvements over simpler or more direct discretization techniques. I therefore recommend a rating of 4: Ok but not good enough – rejection.

1. Originality:  
– The main contribution is to apply stateless actor-critic RL to a graph-partitioning‐based segmentation problem so that non-differentiable priors can be used as rewards. However, the use of RL to handle discrete, non-differentiable objectives is by now fairly standard; the paper builds on off-the-shelf semisoft actor-critic methods [38] and GNN message passing [39].  
– The paper cites Pfau & Vinyals [7] as the primary RL concept, but that work is theoretical; this manuscript’s practical adaptation remains an incremental engineering exercise rather than a fundamentally new RL algorithm.  
– Given the motivation—to optimize discrete assignments under non-differentiable costs—it would strengthen the paper to include empirical comparisons to alternative strategies for handling discreteness in deep nets, such as the straight-through estimator, Gumbel-Softmax relaxations, or DETR-style end-to-end training for set prediction (e.g., Carion et al., “End-to-End Object Detection with Transformers,” arXiv:2005.12872). Without such comparisons, it is unclear whether RL actually outperforms or trades off against these simpler approaches.  
– From the application perspective, the segmentation of light-sheet microscopy images of fruit fly embryos may be novel to some in the computer vision community, but it is a niche domain. A more convincing originality argument would come from testing on a standard instance segmentation benchmark (e.g., COCO, Cityscapes, or the CVPPP leaf segmentation dataset) to show that the RL framework scales beyond microscopy and synthetic circles.

2. Clarity:  
– The Methods section is dense and at times convoluted. For instance, the rationale behind having two “Methods” (Method 1: end-to-end feature extractor; Method 2: pre-trained fixed features) is not clearly motivated. The text should explicitly state why one might choose fixed features (pre-training cost, stability) versus end-to-end learning, and how this choice affects convergence or performance.  
– The decision to use a stateless RL setup (one-step episode length) deserves more explanation. The paper briefly mentions that multi-step setups yielded inferior results (App. §), but the intuition behind why a single step is preferable for graph partitioning would help readers understand the design choices.  
– Figures 4 and 5 use color codings (e.g., reward maps in green/red, PCA projections) that are not intuitively explained for non-microscopy experts. The captions should clearly define what “action=merge” or “action=split” colors represent, and how to interpret the PCA-derived RGB embeddings versus handcrafted feature maps. A small legend inset in each panel would reduce reader confusion.

3. Quality and Significance:  
– The experiments on the synthetic circles dataset show that the RL model can combine a circularity prior with a global object-count prior (via an exponential penalty) to recover disk shapes on a textured background. Baseline mutex watershed fails, but it is not surprising that a high-level Hough-based reward outperforms a purely low-level greedy agglomerator.  
– In the microscopy experiment, the authors report an SBD of 0.482±0.020 versus 0.283±0.023 for the boundary+multicut baseline [47]. While this is a substantial gain, neither the absolute SBD nor the VI numbers (merge VI 0.839, split VI 1.374) can be contextualized: how do standard instance methods such as Mask R-CNN or State-of-the-art embedding losses [28,48] perform if trained with only weak shape priors? Even a quick attempt to fine-tune Mask R-CNN on the 10 annotated images (with data augmentation) would provide a useful reference.  
– The computational overhead of RL (training two GNNs, sampling actions, critic updates, multicut solves per image) is likely substantial compared to a single forward/backward pass in a standard CNN. A runtime or GPU-hours comparison would help quantify the practical tradeoff between extra compute and metric gains.  

4. Additional Questions and Comments:  
– Why choose superpixels as the low-level prior? The paper mentions watershed seeding via external CNN edge detectors [47], but it is not clear whether alternative region proposals (e.g., regular grid cells, learned patch embeddings clustering) might simplify the partitioning step.  
– Could the same high-level priors (shape circularity, bounding-box size, radial distance) be incorporated into a differentiable loss (e.g., via a surrogate approximator as in [17]) to avoid RL altogether? The authors should discuss this alternative.  
– How sensitive is the method to the choice of sub-graph sizes in reward decomposition (Section 3.2)? Some analysis of how performance changes when only one neighborhood scale is used versus four scales (6,12,32,128 edges) would clarify the necessity of overlapping sub-graph rewards.  
– In the microscopy data, cell shapes might deviate from perfect rectangles under certain developmental stages. Does the method degrade gracefully if the shape prior is slightly misspecified? A robustness study would be insightful.

5. Limitations and Societal Impact:  
– The paper currently lacks a dedicated Limitations paragraph. In practice, RL-based training with sparse and noisy rewards is known to be sample-inefficient and prone to local minima. The authors should discuss potential failure modes, e.g., oscillations in edge-weight predictions or overfitting to spurious priors if the assumed object count/shape distribution is wrong.  
– Societal Impact: while biological instance segmentation has positive applications, it may also find downstream use in automated microscopy platforms that could displace some manual annotation jobs. A brief statement acknowledging both potential benefits and caveats (e.g., overreliance on automated priors) would satisfy broader impact requirements.

In summary, the paper presents an interesting construction that stitches together RL, GNNs, and graph partitioning with high-level priors for instance segmentation. However, the novelty is incremental, the methods presentation needs clarification, and the empirical evaluation is limited to niche tasks without comparison to simpler or more widely adopted techniques. I therefore recommend rejection in its current form.

title: Finite-Time Error Bounds for Distributed Linear Stochastic Approximation

reviews.summary: An interesting submission dealing with distributed stochastic approximation driven by Markovian noise, in which the communication topologies considered among agents are captured by a stochastic matrix (in contrast to the doubly stochastic matrix studied in the related literature). Both asymptotic and finite-time error bounds are established and it was shown that the algorithm converges to some unspecified convex combination of the equilibrium points of local tasks. This paper finally proposes a push-type distributed stochastic approximation algorithm and provides its finite-time bounds for the performance by leveraging the analysis for the consensus-type algorithm with stochastic matrices. All analysis and error bounds are directly applicable to distributed TD algorithms.
reviews.main_review:
Originality: This paper studies distributed Markovian stochastic approximation algorithms with stochastic interconnection matrices, which has not yet been covered in the related literature. The results are a bit different than what is known for related algorithms having doubly stochastic interconnection matrices. Further, a push-type algorithm is proposed for decentralized implementation. 

Quality. The results are technically sound and theoretically supported. The algorithm proposed is also new.

Clarity. The paper is well written and easy to follow. The results are nicely organized and the contributions relative to existing works are clear too.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
An interesting submission dealing with distributed stochastic approximation driven by Markovian noise, in which the communication topologies considered among agents are captured by a stochastic matrix (in contrast to the doubly stochastic matrix studied in the related literature). Both asymptotic and finite-time error bounds are established and it was shown that the algorithm converges to some unspecified convex combination of the equilibrium points of local tasks. This paper finally proposes a push-type distributed stochastic approximation algorithm and provides its finite-time bounds for the performance by leveraging the analysis for the consensus-type algorithm with stochastic matrices. All analysis and error bounds are directly applicable to distributed TD algorithms.

Main Review:
Originality: This paper studies distributed Markovian stochastic approximation algorithms with stochastic interconnection matrices, which has not yet been covered in the related literature. The results are a bit different than what is known for related algorithms having doubly stochastic interconnection matrices. Further, a push-type algorithm is proposed for decentralized implementation. 

Quality. The results are technically sound and theoretically supported. The algorithm proposed is also new.

Clarity. The paper is well written and easy to follow. The results are nicely organized and the contributions relative to existing works are clear too.

Significance. The results are important and complement the existing distributed Markovian stochastic approximation results which have only focused on doubly stochastic interconnections among agents. The tools developed for analysis may also be useful for further use.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Numerical simulations may be provided comparing the proposed algorithm and existing ones as well as validating the proved finite-time error bounds for both constant/time-varying stepsizes.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept

reviews.text:
Review:

Summary:  
This submission studies finite-time error bounds for distributed linear stochastic approximation (SA) algorithms over time-varying directed graphs, where the interconnection (consensus) matrices are merely stochastic rather than doubly stochastic. The authors further propose a novel “Push‐SA” algorithm, inspired by the push-sum protocol, which ensures convergence to the straight average of local equilibria under uni‐directional communication. Both constant and time‐varying step‐size regimes are analyzed, and detailed non-asymptotic bounds on the mean-squared error relative to the equilibrium of the associated ODE are provided.

Strengths:  
- The paper significantly relaxes the common bi‐directional communication (doubly stochastic) assumption to the more general setting of stochastic consensus matrices. This generalization is nontrivial, as it breaks down the standard quadratic Lyapunov analysis and requires new tools (absolute probability sequences and quadratic comparison functions).  
- The technical contributions are clearly presented. In particular, Theorems 1 and 2 establish asymptotic consensus and convergence under minimal connectivity assumptions, while Theorem 3 derives explicit finite‐time bounds. The Push‐SA variant is then developed in Section 3, with Theorem 5 providing its own finite‐time error guarantee.  
- The use of absolute probability sequences to handle time‐varying, non-doubly‐stochastic weight matrices is elegant and broadly applicable. By tailoring an “absolute probability sequence” for the push-based algorithm, the authors overcome challenges in bounding both consensus error and single‐agent SA error without requiring full history tracking.  
- The manuscript thoroughly situates itself relative to past work (e.g., [3, 37, 48, 52]) and explains why known analyses fail in the stochastic‐matrix case. The discussion of two main technical challenges—loss of invariance under stochastic weights and lack of quadratic Lyapunov functions—clarifies the novelty of the proof techniques.  
- The proofs are detailed (Appendix B) and account for both constant and diminishing step sizes. The results subsume previous finite‐time analyses that assume doubly stochastic consensus as special cases, and they open the door to practical scenarios with unidirectional links, as in wireless networks or adversarial settings.

Additional Observations on Strengths:  
- The manuscript’s extended discussion in Section 2 on the role of the absolute probability sequence {πₜ} and its limit π∞ neatly explains how the limiting equilibrium becomes an “uncontrollable” convex combination of local equilibria. This insight is critical for understanding why push‐sum is needed to recover the straight average.  
- The paper’s organization helps readers track the flow: Sections 2.1–2.3 build the consensus‐SA framework, while Section 3 introduces Push‐SA and its analysis. The linkage to reinforcement learning (RL), though high‐level, highlights that these linear SA schemes include TD learning as a special case.  
- Numerical constants in the finite‐time bounds (e.g., π_min, β, δ_max, γ_max/min, K₁, K₂) are explicitly characterized, giving practitioners a roadmap for computing or estimating convergence rates in real networks.

Weaknesses:  
- The motivating context centers on applications in reinforcement learning (RL), yet the paper does not explicitly map the proposed algorithms to concrete RL instances such as temporal-difference (TD) learning with function approximation. A brief, but precise, paragraph on how linear SA (equations (1) and (9)) specializes to TD(0) or TD(λ)—including the identification of A, b in terms of feature matrices, transition kernels, and reward vectors—would strengthen the link to RL practitioners. For example, one could note that in policy evaluation, A = ΦᵀD(P – γI)Φ and b = ΦᵀDr, and then explain how multiple agents each hold a local feature set and apply (1) or (9) to cooperatively estimate the global value function.  
- While the finite-time bounds are thorough, the practical evaluation of constants such as K₁, K₂, and C₁–C₁₂ may be challenging. The paper could comment briefly on how one might estimate or bound these quantities in a networked system, or provide a small numerical example in the supplement to illustrate their scale.  
- The manuscript would benefit from a short discussion on computational and communication overheads of Push‐SA compared to standard consensus‐based SA. For instance, Push‐SA requires each agent to maintain and transmit an additional weight variable yᵢₜ per iteration. A complexity comparison (message size, memory per agent) would help readers assess trade‐offs in practice.

Minor Corrections / Typos:  
- Line 36: “arguably” → “inarguably”.  
- Line 37: “differing” → “different”.  

After Rebuttal:  
Thank you for the clarifications in your response. You have addressed several technical questions, yet the key gap remains the explicit RL instantiation (e.g., TD learning mapping). Given the consensus among reviewers and the committee’s detailed feedback, I will maintain my recommendation of marginal rejection.  

Limitations And Societal Impact:  
Yes  

Rating:  
5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold
