reviews.summary: This paper studies a distributed linear stochastic approximation algorithm. The interconnection structure among the agents is described by a time varying directed graph. Under the setting of Markovian samples, the main contribution is to characterize the finite time error bound on the mean squared error. The analysis is applied to study a push-sum version of distributed linear stochastic approximation.
reviews.main_review:
As mentioned, the main contribution is a new analysis on the distributed linear stochastic approximation algorithm given in (1), encompassing constant stepsize and time varying step size, Markovian samples. To achieve this, the study introduces a technique known as the absolute probability sequence to track the evolution of error in light of the time varying directed graph which results in a row-stochastic interconnection matrix. This technique can be interesting as it leads to a simple recursion of the iterates in (4), simplifying the subsequent analysis.

However, there are a number of drawbacks which outweigh the benefits of the current paper, as discussed below:

- Though the paper title reads "distributed linear stochastic approximation", the algorithm in (1) only considers a restricted setting with an identical driving matrix $A(x)$ across agents (and different $b^i(x)$ across agents). This setting may be suitable for the distributed policy evaluation problem where a number of prior works have appeared, but it does not include many interesting cases for distributed linear stochastic approximation including classical ones such as linear regression. In this regard, the paper may better position itself as a paper for distributed policy evaluation insteado of the more general linear stochastic approximation. 

- The main theorems (Theorem 2 & 3) require Assumption 6 which is not well justified. Particularly, this assumption requires the absolute probability seqeunce to admit a limit vector. The Remark 3 which follows the assumption fails to provide a convincing case for this assumption to hold. Therein, two examples are given: the case $W_t$ is doubly stochastic is rather trivial as it defies one of the main relaxation considered in this paper; the case when the number of neighbors of agents are unchanged over time (also, is it the number of out-neighbors? or in-neighbors?) appears to be too artificial. The paper shall include a more concrete construction on how the assumption holds. 

- In addition, it is not clear what are the benefits of employing the absolute probability sequence technique in analyzing the convergence of distributed algorithms, e.g., when compared to the classical approach in [52] for the push-SA algorithm. As for the convergence to a weighted average of the equilibrium point when a row-stochastic mixing matrix is employed, some relevant work can be found in the static graph setting, e.g.,

-- J. Chen, A. Sayed, "Distributed Pareto Optimization via Diffusion Strategies", IEEE Transactions on Signal Processing, 2013. 

- The presentation of the main theorems lacks useful information that a finite-time error analysis should encompass. For instance, it is mentioned that "$C_2$ depends on $L, \gamma_{min}, \gamma_{max}, A_{max}, b_{max}$ ..." which does not describe how small/large the bound is - in fact, without a more detailed description, the bound in (7) is not useful since $C_2$ can be large; the result for push-SA is summarized in Theorem 5 *only* for the case of time varying step size, and it is mentioned that "our current analysis approach cannot be directly applied for this case" which lacks further expatiation; in theorem 3, how does that the convergence rate of $\eta_t$ affects the finite time error bound in the constant step size case? 

---
**Post Rebuttal**

The reviewer appreciates the detailed response from the authors. Unfortunately, the response has not addressed my previous concerns and therefore my previous evaluation stands. In particular, the promised "simulation based" study will not be very convincing since cases which violate Assumption 6 may still include a lot of cases. Instead, it would be better to construct an **analytical** example to illustrate the necessity of Assumption 6. In summary, my main concern is that Assumption 6 and its corresponding analysis due to the "absolute probability sequence" is one of the main innovations claimed in the paper, yet its applications are so far only limited to the previously known cases. 

Moreover, while it is acceptable that $C_2$ can be a large constant, it is also important to point out **how** does $C_2$ depends on the system parameter $L, \gamma_{min}, \gamma_{max}, A_{max}, b_{max}$. The current presentation has so far eluded from discussing about that.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: There isn't any potential negative societal impact. The limitation/suggestions for improvement have been discussed in the main review above.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

reviews.text:
Expanded Review:

Summary of the Paper  
This manuscript studies a distributed linear stochastic approximation (LSA) algorithm over a network of agents whose interconnection is described by a time‐varying directed graph. Each agent updates its local parameter via a consensus‐type mixing step (with a row‐stochastic weight matrix) followed by a local stochastic approximation driven by Markovian noise. The key theoretical contribution is a finite‐time bound on the mean‐square error (MSE) of the iterates relative to the unique equilibrium of the associated ODE, for both constant and diminishing step‐size regimes (Theorem 3). The paper also introduces a push‐sum variant (Algorithm (9)) to achieve a straight‐average combination of local equilibria when only uni‐directional communication is possible, and provides its convergence guarantee (Theorem 5). The analysis hinges on the concept of an “absolute probability sequence” (Definition 2, Lemma 1) to overcome the lack of a quadratic Lyapunov function when the mixing matrices are merely row‐stochastic.

Strengths  
1.   Novelty of Analysis Technique: The use of absolute probability sequences to track the evolution of a weighted Lyapunov‐like function (π_t‐weighted squared norm) is innovative. This method yields the simple SA‐style recursion (4), which decouples the consensus and single‐agent errors.  
2.   Generality of Results: Theorem 3 covers both constant and time‐varying step‐size cases, accommodates Markovian noise under geometric mixing (Assumption 3), and allows arbitrary time‐varying directed graphs that are uniformly strongly connected (Definition 1).  
3.   Push‐Sum Extension: The push‐sum based Algorithm (9) is a useful variant for networks with uni‐directional links. The authors derive new finite‐time MSE bounds (Theorem 5) that, to our knowledge, are the first for push‐based stochastic approximation under Markovian sampling.  
4.   Thorough Technical Writing: The paper is mostly well organized, with clear notation (Section 2), precise assumptions (Assumptions 1–6), and comprehensive appendices (Appendix A, B) that detail the constants (K₁, K₂, C₁–C₁₂) and proof sketches.

Weaknesses  
Despite the above positive aspects, there are several significant drawbacks that limit the impact and applicability of the work:

1.   Overly Restrictive Problem Setting:  
     •  Identical Local A‐Matrices Only. Although titled “distributed linear stochastic approximation,” the algorithm (1) assumes an identical driving matrix A(x) across all agents (Section 2, Assumption 2) and only allows heterogeneity in the bias term bᵢ(x). This setting corresponds to policy evaluation in multi‐agent RL but excludes classical LSA problems such as distributed linear regression or multi‐agent LQR, where each agent may have a different feature matrix.  
     •  Misleading Title and Scope. Given this restriction, the paper would be better framed as “distributed policy evaluation” rather than general LSA. Without extending to heterogeneous Aᵢ(x), the claims about “distributed LSA” are too broad.

2.   Unjustified Assumption 6 (Existence of Limiting Absolute Probability Sequence):  
     •  Key Role in Theorems 2 & 3. Assumption 6 (that the absolute probability sequence π_t converges to a limit π_∞) is required for asymptotic consensus (Theorem 2) and the finite‐time bound (Theorem 3). However, no general conditions on {W_t} are given to guarantee this convergence beyond trivial cases.  
     •  Weak Examples. Remark 3 cites two examples: (a) W_t doubly stochastic—trivial since this violates the main relaxation of the paper; (b) fixed neighbor counts—unclear whether this refers to in‐ or out‐degree, and it is not obvious why this guarantees π_t→π_∞. These examples are too artificial to cover realistic directed networks with changing topology.  
     •  Need for Analytical Counterexample. To convincingly justify Assumption 6, the paper should construct an explicit analytical example of {W_t} where π_t fails to converge and show that, indeed, the iterates θᵢ_t do not converge or the finite‐time bounds blow up. This would illustrate the necessity of Assumption 6.

3.   Unclear Benefits of the Absolute Probability Sequence Technique:  
     •  Relation to Classical Analyses. The authors should compare their approach explicitly with existing analyses of push‐sum methods in distributed optimization, e.g., [52] (Nedić & Olshevsky 2014), or the diffusion‐Pareto approach in J. Chen & A. Sayed (2013). In particular, it remains unclear whether the absolute probability sequence leads to tighter bounds or conceptual simplifications over standard approaches that directly track weighted averages.  
     •  Static Graph Literature Overlooked. Prior static‐graph LSA analyses (e.g., Chen & Sayed, “Distributed Pareto Optimization via Diffusion Strategies,” IEEE Trans. Sig. Proc. 2013) establish convergence to a weighted combination of equilibria under row‐stochastic mixing. The manuscript should discuss how its results extend or differ from these static‐graph analyses when the graph is time‐varying.

4.   Missing Quantitative Interpretation of Finite‐Time Constants:  
     •  Large, Implicit Constants. The constant C₂ in (7) is said to depend on L, γ_min, γ_max, A_max, b_max (Remark 4), but no explicit expression or scaling is provided. Without understanding how C₂ grows with system size N, network diameter, or conditioning of P, the finite‐time bound is not interpretable or useful in practice.  
     •  Influence of η_t on Error Bound. In Theorem 3, the rate at which η_t = ∥π_t–π_∞∥² decays is critical for the MSE bound under constant step‐size, yet the paper does not quantify the impact of η_t. How does a slower convergence of π_t affect the term C₂ or the exponential decay rate? This connection should be made precise.  
     •  Push‐SA Only for Diminishing Step‐Size. Theorem 5 provides the push‐sum result only for α_t=α₀/(t+1). The statement “our current analysis approach cannot be directly applied for the fixed step‐size case” (p. 22) lacks further explanation. Why does the proof break down, and can it be patched? Without a fixed step‐size result, the push‐SA analysis is incomplete.

Suggestions for Improvement  
–  Broaden the Algorithmic Scope. Extend Algorithm (1) to allow heterogeneous Aᵢ(x) across agents, or explicitly restrict the paper’s focus to policy‐evaluation settings.  
–  Justify Assumption 6 Analytically. Include a concrete directed‐graph example (e.g., a 3‐agent cycle with changing mixing weights) in which π_t fails to converge, and demonstrate numerically or analytically that the algorithm indeed fails without Assumption 6.  
–  Clarify the Role of Absolute Probability Sequences. Compare in detail the new analysis against standard push‐sum convergence proofs (e.g., [52], [63]) and highlight any quantitative advantages or simplifications.  
–  Quantify Finite‐Time Constants. Provide explicit (or at least order‐of‐magnitude) formulas for C₂, C₆, etc., to show how the bound scales with network size N, mixing rate β, diameter L, and ODE conditioning γ_max/γ_min.  
–  Address Fixed Step‐Size Push‐SA. Either extend the analysis for a constant α or explain in the main text why the diminishing‐steps analysis cannot be adapted.

Decision  
5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold. While the paper introduces an interesting technical tool (absolute probability sequences) and addresses a meaningful relaxation (row‐stochastic mixing) in distributed LSA, the restrictive setting (identical A), the unsubstantiated Assumption 6, and the lack of quantitative clarity on finite‐time constants significantly limit the work’s impact. The manuscript requires substantial revision and additional analytical evidence before it can meet the bar for acceptance.

Moreover, while it is acceptable that $C_2$ can be a large constant, it is also important to point out **how** does $C_2$ depends on the system parameter $L, \gamma_{min}, \gamma_{max}, A_{max}, b_{max}$. The current presentation has so far eluded from discussing about that.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
There isn't any potential negative societal impact. The limitation/suggestions for improvement have been discussed in the main review above.

Rating:
5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold

This paper consider the setting in which the agents interact over a time-varying directed graph, which extend previous studies. Overall the results in this paper is solid and has some technical contributions.
reviews.main_review:
Overall I think the results in this paper is solid and should be some interesting to the community. I have the following comments about the paper.

(1) Only convergence rate is characterized in the main theorems. It would be better to see the sample complexity result (total number of samples that required to achieve $\epsilon$-accuracy level) so that the readers can compare the contributions in this paper with the state-of-art.

(2) Although it is a theoretical paper, empirical verification is also suggested even with toy example.

(3) As claimed by the author, the dependence on some conditional numbers is not clear. However those conditional numbers could affects the convergence rate significantly. A detailed discussion about those numbers would makes the paper more convincing.

title: Local $K$-means: An Efficient Optimization Algorithm And Its Generalization

reviews.summary: This paper introduces a local efficient version of k-means which leverages the KNN graph of the data to compute neighborhoods of data. Extensive empirical results both on synthetic and real data are reported.
reviews.main_review:
Running times are not reported for the results on real data in Table 3. One of the main advantages of the proposed method is gained efficiency with respect to the clustering competitors, therefore running times should be reported and discussed. 

The proposed method LKM also depends on the initialization of the clustering y, and this is not discussed. It's unclear whether the results on simulated data in Table 1 are averages of different runs or not. This is not discussed and it should. Results should be averages and standard deviations should also be reported.

It is well-known that k-means is sensitive to outliers, but it's unclear in Figure 3(b) why the performance of k-means is poor in this case. It seems it was still able to detect the four clusters in the data?

In Section 3, the definition of neighborhood of a point is confusing and not necessarily correct. The notion of neighbor is not symmetric: x_j may be a neighbor of x_i, but not vice versa. 

I don't find the analogy of KNN graph with social networks particularly useful or insightful. They are not quite the same concept, really. The paper can instead be improved by discussing related work on exemplar-based approaches aimed at making clustering more robust. See for example recent work on regularized NMF methods which leverage the KNN graphs of the data, e.g. Mani et al, Unsupervised Selective Manifold Regularized Matrix Factorization, SDM 2021, and related work.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Limitations related to the construction of the KNN graph are discussed. No potential negative societal impact is expected.

reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper presents Local K-Means (LKM), a variant of the classical k-means algorithm that replaces global distance computations with local distances defined on a k-nearest neighbor (k-NN) graph. By taking as input only the distances between each sample and its k neighbors (and using a constant λ for all other pairwise distances), the authors derive an objective (Eq. 8) that is robust to outliers and admits an efficient update scheme (Algorithm 1) with time complexity O(nk). They further show that LKM, KSUMS, and ratio‐cut spectral clustering can all be formulated as special cases of a unified model (Eq. 11). Extensive experiments on eleven synthetic datasets and sixteen real benchmarks compare LKM to k-means, spectral clustering, RCC, FINCH, AGCI, FCDMF, and KSUMS, demonstrating competitive accuracy while requiring only O(nk) time and space.

Strengths:
- The unification of LKM, KSUMS, and SC under a single objective (Section 3.1) is elegant and clarifies connections among these algorithms.  
- Algorithm 1 leverages the sparsity of the local dissimilarity matrix G(k) to achieve an O(nk) optimization, which is appealing for large-scale or high–cluster‐count scenarios.  
- The synthetic experiments (Figures 2–4 and Table 1–2) systematically assess effectiveness on the “Mickey” and “Outlier” setups and measure speedups over k-means under varying dataset sizes and standard deviations.  
- The paper discusses the limitation that LKM requires a k-NN graph, noting that approximate graph construction can be challenging in very high dimensions.

Weaknesses:
1. Real‐data running times are not reported. Table 3 lists ACC, NMI, and ARI for sixteen benchmarks but omits any timing comparisons. Since one of LKM’s main claims is improved efficiency over competitors, the absence of wall‐clock times, iteration counts, or preprocessing costs on real datasets leaves a gap in validating its practical speed advantages.
2. The role of initialization for LKM is not addressed. Algorithm 1 begins with “Initialize y randomly,” yet the manuscript does not state whether results in Table 1 or Table 3 are averaged over multiple random starts, nor does it report standard deviations. As performance in discrete‐assignment methods often varies with initialization, reporting means and error bars over, say, 10–50 seeds is vital for reproducibility and to assess robustness.
3. In Fig. 3(b), the text claims that k-means “is severely affected by the outlier A,” but visually k-means still segments four clusters around the intended centers. It would help to clarify whether cluster centers shifted, which points were misassigned, and to quantify degradation (e.g., precision/recall) in this example.
4. The definition of Nk(xi) in Section 3 (lines 85–94) combines “i is among j’s k nearest neighbors or vice versa,” leading to an asymmetric notion of neighborhood. A clearer, symmetric definition or justification is needed, since the optimization and complexity analysis (Section 3.2) rely on |Nk(xi)| ≈ 2k.
5. The analogy to social‐network communities in Figure 1 is not particularly illuminating, as typical graph‐based clustering approaches already make similar connections. Instead, the paper could strengthen its related work section by discussing exemplar‐based clustering methods that leverage k-NN graphs for robustness—e.g., Mani et al.’s Unsupervised Selective Manifold Regularized Matrix Factorization (SDM 2021)—or other recent regularized NMF approaches.

Suggestions for Improvement:
- Add a column in Table 3 (or a separate table) reporting total runtime, number of iterations to converge, and graph–construction time for each real dataset.  
- Clarify initialization—state how many restarts were used, present average metrics with standard deviations, and discuss convergence behavior in practice.  
- Provide quantitative metrics (e.g., cluster center shift magnitude or misassignment counts) for the “Outlier” example to back up the robustness claim.  
- Revise the neighborhood definition to enforce symmetry, and explain how this affects the sparsity and correctness of the proposed optimization.  
- Expand the related work discussion to include exemplar‐based and manifold‐regularized clustering techniques that also exploit k-NN structures.

Limitations and Societal Impact:
The authors have acknowledged the need for constructing an accurate k-NN graph when features are high-dimensional, which can be a bottleneck. No negative societal impacts are apparent.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

This work introduces a useful, efficient variant of k-means and provides a cohesive theoretical framework, but would benefit from more thorough empirical validation of its claimed speed‐ups and from clarifying key methodological details.

reviews.summary: This submission introduces a computationally efficient clustering algorithm called LKM, which focuses on the scenarios where data is presented in the form of graph. In brief, only the k-nearest neighbour distances of each point are considered in the objective and a simple truncation of large distances is introduced to mitigate the effect of outliers.  The method is shown to produce promising practical performance in comparison with existing techniques both computationally and in terms of clustering performance.
reviews.main_review:
Strengths:

LKM is developed based on the k-means problem which is a fundamental problem in machine learning and the authors might bring some new insights and develop a new algorithm. The main strength of LKM lies in the practical aspects of the proposed algorithm, in that it is efficient and the performance is compelling. 
Specifically, the computational overhead is O(nk), where n and k denote the number of samples and nearest neighbors, respectively.

In addition, the unified framework of KSUMSC, and LCM also seems interesting. It revolves that SC, a popular clustering algorithm derived from graph cuts, can be optimized with the same time complexity as that of LKM.

Weaknesses:
1. Line 9: "Specifically....are denote the...". The sentence is grammatically incorrect, "are" should be removed.

2. Some works relating to the connection between k-means and clustering with graph cuts are not discussed in Section 2 (Related work).

3. The performance of SC optimized by the proposed algorithm is not shown.

4. What does \bar{y} stand for in Eq. (15) and (16)?

5. e_i should be a column vector.

6. Some steps in the derivations are not explained. What is the connection between (11) and (17)? 

7. The evaluation needs to be improved. The proposed clustering method uses ball-tree to construct a k-nearest neighbors graph as an intermediate step. Correspondingly, it is suggested to include the clustering performance of ball-tree as a baseline to compare.

8. Have the paper proof-read to remove grammatical errors

9. In page 7, line 178, "#Iter" should be "#Iter.".

10. In page 7, table 2, "FLK" should be "LKM".

11. References should be shown in the same format.

**comment after rebuttal**
I acknowledge that I read and other reviewers' comments and authors' responses. I would like to keep my score as most concerns of mine have been well addressed.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes.

reviews.text:
Review for “Local K-means: An Efficient Optimization Algorithm and Its Generalization”

Summary  
This paper proposes Local K-Means (LKM), a novel clustering algorithm designed to operate on graph-form data by leveraging only k-nearest neighbour (k-NN) distances and applying a simple truncation strategy to downweight distant (potentially outlier) points. The authors further present a unified model that encompasses LKM, the KSUMS algorithm, and spectral clustering (SC), and they derive an alternating optimization procedure that runs in O(nk) time per full pass—where n is the number of data points and k the number of neighbours. Extensive experiments on eleven synthetic and sixteen benchmark datasets demonstrate that LKM matches or exceeds the clustering accuracy of strong baselines (e.g., k-means, KSUMS, SC) while dramatically reducing runtime, especially when the number of clusters is large. Overall, I find the practical contributions compelling and the empirical evaluation thorough.

Strengths  
1. Practical Efficiency. LKM achieves an attractive time complexity of O(nk) per iteration (Section 3.2, Algorithm 1), which is asymptotically better than the O(nc) cost of standard Lloyd’s k-means when the number of clusters c is comparable to k or when c is large. The authors convincingly show in Table 2 that LKM (including the time to build the k-NN graph via Ball-Tree) can be 5–11× faster per iteration than k-means on grid-like synthetic datasets and retains this advantage on larger, real-world benchmark sets.  
2. Unified Framework. The observation that LKM, KSUMS, and spectral clustering can all be cast as instances of the optimization problem in Eq. (11) is insightful. By introducing the parameter p (Section 3.1) and the choice of the graph weight matrix G(k), the paper elegantly shows how these three methods sit on a common spectrum. This theoretical unification could inspire future work that interpolates between the purely local (LKM) and purely global (ratio-cut) regimes.  
3. Robustness to Outliers. The truncation of distances not in each point’s k-NN set to a constant λ (Eq. 9) is a simple yet effective way to mitigate the influence of isolated outliers (demonstrated on the “Outlier” synthetic data in Figure 3). Unlike classical k-means, where an extreme point can drag a cluster center far away, LKM treats non-neighbours uniformly and therefore remains stable.  
4. Empirical Validation. The authors deliver a wide-ranging empirical study: (i) on synthetic Mickey and Outlier data to illustrate effectiveness and robustness; (ii) on scalable grid-based data to highlight runtime and accuracy trade-offs; (iii) on sixteen diverse benchmarks (face, text, image, bioinformatics) where LKM ranks at or near the top in ACC, NMI, and ARI (Table 3). Such a comprehensive evaluation builds confidence in the method’s applicability.

Weaknesses and Suggestions  
1. Minor grammatical and typographical errors.  
   - In the Introduction (line 9), “where n and k are denote the number of samples and nearest neighbours, respectively” should read “where n and k denote….”.  
   - In Algorithm 1 and the surrounding text (page 7), “#Iter” should consistently be “#Iter.” and the table header “FLK” should be corrected to “LKM.”  
   - The reference list uses inconsistent formatting (e.g., some entries display full first names, others initials). A uniform citation style should be adopted.  
   Recommendation: A careful proof-read to fix these issues will improve presentation.  

2. Missing connections to related work on k-means and graph cuts.  
   While Section 2 surveys methods that accelerate k-means (e.g., Ball k-means [32], coreset methods [7]) and fast spectral algorithms [33, 20, 9], it omits earlier studies that explicitly link k-means objectives to graph-cut formulations (e.g., works on kernel k-means and normalized cuts, or the equivalence between ratio association and weighted k-means). Incorporating and contrasting these threads would strengthen the positioning of the unified model in Section 3.1.

3. Presentation of spectral clustering optimization.  
   The paper claims that SC can be optimized with the same O(nk) complexity via the unified update (Section 3.2), but does not show any empirical results for this accelerated SC. Reporting runtime and accuracy for “fast SC” (i.e., the p=1, g(k) version of Eq. 11) on a few benchmarks would substantiate the claim that spectral clustering can indeed benefit as much as LKM from this approach.

4. Clarification of notation in Eqs. (15)–(17).  
   In Eq. (15) and (16), the notation ¯yᵢ (= y_i?) is used without definition. A reader cannot readily tell whether ¯yᵢ is a row of Y or an indicator aggregate. Similarly, in Eq. (18), the definition of eᵢ should specify that it is a standard basis column vector in Rᶜ (not a row). Providing a brief reminder of these conventions (e.g., “Let y_i denote the i-th row of Y as a column vector…”) would eliminate confusion.

5. Gaps in derivation between Eqs. (11) and (17).  
   The leap from the general trace form in Eq. (11) to the per-cluster contributions in Eq. (17) is not fully exposited. A few sentences or a short supplemental derivation explaining how the trace and inverse norms break down into the sums over s_i and n_i terms would be helpful for readers to follow the optimization logic.

6. Baseline for graph construction cost.  
   Since LKM relies on a k-NN graph precomputed by Ball-Tree, it would be fair to compare against algorithms that also require or could benefit from this graph input. For example, applying a standard graph-cut clustering directly on the same Ball-Tree k-NN graph (e.g., ratio-cut or NCut with k neighbors) would serve as a meaningful baseline for runtime vs. accuracy. This would isolate improvements due to the local k-means objective from those solely due to graph sparsity.

7. Proof-reading of supplementary materials.  
   The supplementary section reportedly contains the detailed sparse‐matrix implementation, which is crucial for reproducing the O(nk) claims. A cursory check suggests minor typos there as well (e.g., missing algorithm labels). Giving it the same copy-editing attention will ensure clarity for implementers.

Conclusion  
In summary, this paper addresses a timely problem—efficient clustering on large data graphs—by introducing a principled variant of k-means that only considers local neighbour distances and truncates the rest. The theoretical unification with KSUMS and spectral clustering adds depth to the contribution, while the O(nk) algorithm and thorough experiments confirm practical gains. The suggested improvements primarily concern exposition and the inclusion of a few additional baselines or runtimes, rather than fundamental flaws. I therefore recommend acceptance (Rating = 7: Good paper, accept).

reviews.summary: The authors use a reformulation of the k-means objective to propose an algorithm that minimizes that objective (or some relaxation thereof), and then show some experiments that indicate promising clustering performance.  Currently I believe the paper needs revision before acceptance: the empirical results are not particularly compelling as they omit runtimes on real-world datasets, and the theoretical results do not factor in or bound the number of iterations of the algorithm.
reviews.main_review:
Hello authors,

Thank you for your submission.  I enjoyed reading it and I think there are some nice ideas contained in the paper.  However, I have a number of concerns that keep me from recommending acceptance for this paper.  Primarily, my biggest concern is the given runtime bound (which I find inaccurate as it does not count the number of iterations), and the experimental evaluation: runtime analysis is a very important measure of the utility of an algorithm, and this is missing for the real-world datasets.

More details below (in no particular order):

1. The computational complexity does not consider the number of iterations before convergence.  Therefore I find the claim that the algorithm takes $O(nc)$ time (or rather $O(n(k + c))$ where $c$ is the number of clusters and $k$ is the number of distances known per point) misleading or incorrect.  In fact, Lloyd's algorithm also takes $O(nc)$ per iteration, but the number of iterations is not easily bounded.  Unless the authors can conclusively show that the number of iterations should not be considered in the runtime, this claim must be modified or retracted.

2. As pointed out in the introduction, the initialization of k-means can make a huge difference for performance.  However, on line 204 the authors simply write "K-means is initialized in a random way"---but what is this random way?  If I were to, e.g., randomly assign points to clusters to initialize, this can lead to very poor results and slow convergence, at least for Lloyd's algorithm.  Even
running many trials of this strategy will produce poor performance for every strategy.  Thus proper initialization is very important.  It would be very helpful if the authors could provide more information on this point.

3. The authors have not provided any information on runtime on real-world datasets.  Thus it is difficult to evaluate the utility of the proposed algorithm, LKM.  Clustering algorithms often behave very differently on diverse datasets, and thus I think that runtimes only on the toy dataset---and only comparing with Lloyd's algorithm---does not give a full picture.  In fact, the number of iterations on the toy datasets is very small when compared to what Lloyd's algorithm will do on higher-dimensional real datasets (where cluster boundaries are not so clear-cut).  For the sake of clarity, the authors should address the issue of runtime comprehensively on real-world datasets.

4. It would also be nice to include some information on the dimensionality and number of points in each dataset listed in 4.2.1 in the main paper, if possible.  I appreciate that 16 datasets are used---this seems like a fairly comprehensive set of experiments---but I suspect most readers will not have an idea of what the characteristics of these datasets are, and many will not look at the
supplemental material.  When I consult the supplementary material, I see that many of the datasets used are relatively high-dimensional; the minimum dimension appears to be 256.  I would suggest that the authors also try on lower-dimensional data (e.g. below 50 dimensions), as convergence characteristics of different algorithms tend to be very different in lower dimensions.

5. A note about the limitations: there are many techniques to quickly construct k-NN graphs.  In lower dimensions, this can be done via trees (see, e.g., [1-3]); in higher dimensions, this can be done approximately via hashing (e.g., [4-5]).  In fact even if only a similarity function is used, so long as that function is a kernel, additional branch-and-bound techniques could be used ([6-8]).  Anyway, perhaps you will find these interesting, if you were not already aware of those lines of work.

6. When comparing Lloyd's algorithm to LKM, note also that there are several variants of Lloyd's algorithm that produce exactly the same result in accelerated time.  The authors even cite some (reference [11]).  There are more however, especially Elkan's [9] and Hamerly's [10] that perform very well in practice that should probably be considered in an empirical comparison.  Personally, if I am doing k-means in practice, I often don't bother with the standard Lloyd algorithm because Elkan's or Hamerly's variants (or the
tree-based ones) are so much faster---it can often be an orders-of-magnitude speedup.

7. Can more detail be provided in Algorithm 1 on how y is initialized randomly?  Is the algorithm uniformly randomly drawing a cluster assignment for each point?

8. I think the paper could benefit from some careful proofreading.  There are numerous grammatical issues, especially having to do with tenses (present/past/etc.).

9. The suggestion in Eq. (10) to convert a (non-metric) similarity function to a dissimilarity function still produces a non-metric: it is not guaranteed to satisfy the triangle inequality.  This makes the change from Eq. (8) (which is built on distances using D) to Eq. (11) (which is built on dissimilarities using G) significant: particularly, I am no longer certain that we are solving the same problem as (3).  I am okay with the fact that LKM solves a relaxation of (3) but I think this should be explicitly pointed out.  On the other hand, note that if the similarity function is a kernel, then it *does* induce a distance metric in kernel space, and that could be used instead.  Specifically, Eq. (2.2) in [8] uses this induced distance metric.

[2] "Tree-independent dual-tree algorithms", ICML 2013
[3] "Random projection trees and low dimensional manifolds", STOC 2008
[4] "Near-optimal hashing algorithms for approximate nearest neighbor in high
dimensions", FOCS '06
[5] "LSH Forest: Practical Algorithms Made Theoretical", SODA 2017
[6] "Asymmetric LSH (ALSH) for sublinear time maximum inner product search (MIPS)", NIPS 2014
[7] "Maximum inner-product search using cone trees", KDD 2012
[8] "Fast Exact Max-Kernel Search", SDM 2013
[9] "Using the triangle inequality to accelerate k-means", ICML 2003
[10] "Making k-means even faster", SDM 2010

I hope that these comments are helpful!

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The authors use a reformulation of the k-means objective to propose an algorithm that minimizes that objective (or some relaxation thereof), and then show some experiments that indicate promising clustering performance.  Currently I believe the paper needs revision before acceptance: the empirical results are not particularly compelling as they omit runtimes on real-world datasets, and the theoretical results do not factor in or bound the number of iterations of the algorithm.

Main Review:
Hello authors,

Thank you for your submission.  I enjoyed reading it and I think there are some nice ideas contained in the paper.  However, I have a number of concerns that keep me from recommending acceptance for this paper.  Primarily, my biggest concern is the given runtime bound (which I find inaccurate as it does not count the number of iterations), and the experimental evaluation: runtime analysis is a very important measure of the utility of an algorithm, and this is missing for the real-world datasets.

More details below (in no particular order):

1. The computational complexity does not consider the number of iterations before convergence.  Therefore I find the claim that the algorithm takes $O(nc)$ time (or rather $O(n(k + c))$ where $c$ is the number of clusters and $k$ is the number of distances known per point) misleading or incorrect.  In fact, Lloyd's algorithm also takes $O(nc)$ per iteration, but the number of iterations is not easily bounded.  Unless the authors can conclusively show that the number of iterations should not be considered in the runtime, this claim must be modified or retracted.

2. As pointed out in the introduction, the initialization of k-means can make a huge difference for performance.  However, on line 204 the authors simply write "K-means is initialized in a random way"---but what is this random way?  If I were to, e.g., randomly assign points to clusters to initialize, this can lead to very poor results and slow convergence, at least for Lloyd's algorithm.  Even
running many trials of this strategy will produce poor performance for every strategy.  Thus proper initialization is very important.  It would be very helpful if the authors could provide more information on this point.

3. The authors have not provided any information on runtime on real-world datasets.  Thus it is difficult to evaluate the utility of the proposed algorithm, LKM.  Clustering algorithms often behave very differently on diverse datasets, and thus I think that runtimes only on the toy dataset---and only comparing with Lloyd's algorithm---does not give a full picture.  In fact, the number of iterations on the toy datasets is very small when compared to what Lloyd's algorithm will do on higher-dimensional real datasets (where cluster boundaries are not so clear-cut).  For the sake of clarity, the authors should address the issue of runtime comprehensively on real-world datasets.

4. It would also be nice to include some information on the dimensionality and number of points in each dataset listed in 4.2.1 in the main paper, if possible.  I appreciate that 16 datasets are used---this seems like a fairly comprehensive set of experiments---but I suspect most readers will not have an idea of what the characteristics of these datasets are, and many will not look at the
supplemental material.  When I consult the supplementary material, I see that many of the datasets used are relatively high-dimensional; the minimum dimension appears to be 256.  I would suggest that the authors also try on lower-dimensional data (e.g. below 50 dimensions), as convergence characteristics of different algorithms tend to be very different in lower dimensions.

5. A note about the limitations: there are many techniques to quickly construct k-NN graphs.  In lower dimensions, this can be done via trees (see, e.g., [1-3]); in higher dimensions, this can be done approximately via hashing (e.g., [4-5]).  In fact even if only a similarity function is used, so long as that function is a kernel, additional branch-and-bound techniques could be used ([6-8]).  Anyway, perhaps you will find these interesting, if you were not already aware of those lines of work.

6. When comparing Lloyd's algorithm to LKM, note also that there are several variants of Lloyd's algorithm that produce exactly the same result in accelerated time.  The authors even cite some (reference [11]).  There are more however, especially Elkan's [9] and Hamerly's [10] that perform very well in practice that should probably be considered in an empirical comparison.  Personally, if I am doing k-means in practice, I often don't bother with the standard Lloyd algorithm because Elkan's or Hamerly's variants (or the
tree-based ones) are so much faster---it can often be an orders-of-magnitude speedup.

7. Can more detail be provided in Algorithm 1 on how y is initialized randomly?  Is the algorithm uniformly randomly drawing a cluster assignment for each point?

8. I think the paper could benefit from some careful proofreading.  There are numerous grammatical issues, especially having to do with tenses (present/past/etc.).

9. The suggestion in Eq. (10) to convert a (non-metric) similarity function to a dissimilarity function still produces a non-metric: it is not guaranteed to satisfy the triangle inequality.  This makes the change from Eq. (8) (which is built on distances using D) to Eq. (11) (which is built on dissimilarities using G) significant: particularly, I am no longer certain that we are solving the same problem as (3).  I am okay with the fact that LKM solves a relaxation of (3) but I think this should be explicitly pointed out.  On the other hand, note that if the similarity function is a kernel, then it *does* induce a distance metric in kernel space, and that could be used instead.  Specifically, Eq. (2.2) in [8] uses this induced distance metric.

[1] "N-Body'problems in statistical learning", NIPS 2001
[2] "Tree-independent dual-tree algorithms", ICML 2013
[3] "Random projection trees and low dimensional manifolds", STOC 2008
[4] "Near-optimal hashing algorithms for approximate nearest neighbor in high
dimensions", FOCS '06
[5] "LSH Forest: Practical Algorithms Made Theoretical", SODA 2017
[6] "Asymmetric LSH (ALSH) for sublinear time maximum inner product search (MIPS)", NIPS 2014
[7] "Maximum inner-product search using cone trees", KDD 2012
[8] "Fast Exact Max-Kernel Search", SDM 2013
[9] "Using the triangle inequality to accelerate k-means", ICML 2003
[10] "Making k-means even faster", SDM 2010

I hope that these comments are helpful!

Limitations And Societal Impact:
I don't think that a new clustering algorithm has either positive or negative societal impact.  So everything seems fine here.

4: Ok but not good enough - rejection

reviews.original_rating: 4: Ok but not good enough - rejection

reviews.summary: This paper considers a variant of k-means which takes the k-nearest neighbor graph as input. The proposed algorithm, called local k-means (LKM in short), then works by locally optimizing the assignment vector of a point using an objective function that is roughly the incremental cost for the k-means objective. The paper provided a lot of empirical data to show the advantages of LKM.
reviews.main_review:
Overall, this paper proposes a novel k-means algorithm, but I think there has not been enough discussion on its guarantees (from both a theoretical and empircal point of view) to make it convincing. The paper writing can also be greatly improved.

Originality: The mthods used in this paper are new. The paper converts the k-means objective into a different format which is compatible with nearest-neighbor graph inputs, where the main variable is the indicator matrix, and optimizes the columns in this matrix one by one (each column corresponding to the assignment vector of one point). The idea of optimizing the assignment of each point to clusters might not be new to the community, but the proposed local optization function seems to be novel.

Quality: The paper does not provide any theoretical guarantees for the proposed algorithm and mainly used empirical evaluation approaches to show its superiority. The experiments are carefully designed, but I think the authors are trying to make some claims that are too broad and thus the provided empirical results do not seem to be convincing enough to show that this approach really beats state-of-the-art. LKM is compared with a lot of baselines and that may still not cover the entire state-of-the-art clustering approaches. Some comments:
- The self-synthesized data are well designed, and serves to show that LKM performs well on some datasets whose natural clustering cannot be captured by the optimal k-means objective function. However, it seems to me that they might be suitable for other types of clustering such as density-based clustering (DBSCAN might be good for "Mickey"), or common k-means implementation packages might also be good for them (k-means++ might be good for "Outlier" depending on the sampled seeds?).  I wonder if the authors have considered these?
- After reading the experiment section I get a bit more confused about the claims that this paper is trying to make: LKM seems to be beating most baselines on the datasets in clustering quality and be significantly faster than k-means? The improvement of LKM , especially when compared with KSUMS on most of the datasets, do not seem to be very significant. Actually, the results gave me the impression that there are many datasets and each of them might be suitable for different types of clustering methods, with LKM performing relative well for all datasets.

Clarity: The writing clarity is fine, although occasionally there are some flaws such as definition/notation that has not been used again later, typos and so on, but it does not affect reading too much. It has a clear problem definition and algorithm description. That being said, I'm not very impressed with the organization of the paper. 
- The paper spent enough space on problem definition but the paragraphs did not serve to fully motivate this design. For example, previous methods like KSUMS and LKM, whose math formulation, as the authors pointed out, have very similar structure with the one used in this paper, but the authors did not provide intuition about why the differences lead to better performance.
- The description of the algorithm design is only about one page and I think there should be more intuitive explanation of why this particular design of local objective function (18) might lead to a better solution and make it robust against outliers. 

Significance: This paper could potentially propose a solution that advances state-of-the-art, but the comparsion between the new algorithm and previous approaches does not seem to convince people of its superiority. Also, I'm not sure if the improvement is significant enough for this venue.

Minor comments:
- In abstract, line 5, the authors mentioned "KSUMS and SC" without citation and before defining which algorithms these are referring to.
- line 26, "For matrix A, we call it indicator matrix", I don't really see the point of introducing the indicator matrix here, and the notation is not really used again in this paper.
- line 86, "where diag(A) = [a11,..., ann]^T",  A is not used in (7), and I'm not sure about what a11, ..., ann are referring to.
- In (9), I'm not sure about why is d_ij^{(k)} set as \gamma otherwise? Can the authors comment on this?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: The authors have adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work.

Rating:
5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review for “Local K-means: An Efficient Optimization Algorithm And Its Generalization”

Summary:  
This paper introduces Local K-Means (LKM), a clustering algorithm that adapts the classical k-means framework to operate on a k-nearest neighbor (k-NN) graph input. Rather than computing distances to all current cluster centroids, LKM optimizes the assignment of one data point at a time by evaluating a local incremental cost function derived from the k-means objective (see Eq. (18) in the manuscript). The authors also present a unifying model (Eq. (11)) that connects LKM to KSUMS [23] and spectral clustering (SC), and they develop an alternating‐update optimization procedure (Algorithm 1) achieving O(nk) per-iteration time complexity. Extensive experiments on eleven synthetic and sixteen benchmark datasets compare LKM against seven baselines (AGCI, FINCH, k-means, KSUMS, RCC, SC, and FCDMF), demonstrating competitive accuracy and faster runtime for large-scale problems.

Overall Recommendation: 5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold  
While the paper tackles an interesting adaptation of k-means for graph inputs and shows promising empirical results, there remain open questions regarding its theoretical guarantees, the breadth of empirical comparisons, and clarity in presentation. I recommend a “marginal reject” primarily because the scope of claims is broader than what the evidence supports, and key explanations (intuition, motivation, and parameter effects) are underdeveloped.

1. Originality  
  • The idea of local updates—reassigning one point at a time using a cost increment—is reminiscent of classic k-means variants, but the specific formulation of the local objective in Eq. (18) (with terms b_j from Eq. (20)) appears novel and tailored to the k-NN graph setting.  
  • Deriving the unified model in Eq. (11) that bridges LKM, KSUMS (Eq. (12)), and Ratio‐cut (Eq. (13)) is an interesting conceptual contribution. The introduction of the parameter p in the unified form suggests potential for future exploration.  
  • The linear‐time O(nk) optimization routine (Algorithm 1 with update rules in Eqs. (21)–(22)) is a practical advance, especially compared to O(n²) spectral methods.  

2. Quality  
  • The paper lacks any theoretical analysis of convergence (e.g., to a local minimum) or approximation bounds relative to the global k-means objective or the unified model in Eq. (11). Without such guarantees, readers must rely solely on empirical evidence.  
  • Empirical evaluation is extensive in terms of dataset variety, but in my view the authors overstate the breadth of “state‐of‐the‐art” baselines. For example, density‐based methods (e.g., DBSCAN) could capture the “Mickey” structure much as LKM does, and k-means++ initialization might similarly mitigate the influence of a single outlier on the “Outlier” dataset. Direct comparisons to such algorithms would clarify to what extent LKM’s success stems from its local‐graph perspective versus general robustness properties of other methods.  
  • In Table 3 on real data (e.g., LFW, CALFW, Olivetti), LKM outperforms alternatives by a few percentage points in ACC/NMI/ARI, but the margin over KSUMS or SC is sometimes marginal (e.g., 0.5–2%). The paper would benefit from statistical tests (paired t-test or bootstrap confidence intervals) to assess significance.  
  • Runtime comparisons (Table 2) convincingly show LKM’s per-iteration speed on synthetic “Grid” family datasets, but for the large benchmark sets it would help to report total wall-clock time, number of iterations to convergence, and memory overhead for constructing the k-NN graph (ball-tree or approximate).  

3. Clarity  
  • Notational issues: in Sect. 1.1 the indicator‐matrix A is introduced but hardly used; similar for diag(A) in Eq. (7) where A is undefined and the meaning of entries a_{ii} is unclear. These stray definitions distract from the main thread.  
  • The model derivation from Eq. (5) through Eq. (8) is algebraically correct but low on intuition. A schematic example (perhaps a 1D toy with k=2) illustrating how local distances weigh into the assignment cost would greatly aid understanding.  
  • The choice to clamp distances to γ when points are not mutual k-NN (Eq. (9)) is not motivated. Why is a constant penalization preferable to ignoring those edges or using the true distance? A brief discussion of alternatives (e.g., sparsification thresholds, learned weights) would be helpful.  
  • Algorithm 1 refers to “sparse matrix G^{(k)} ∈ ℝ^{n×n}” though G^{(k)} actually has at most 2nk non-default entries. A clearer statement in the text and pseudo‐code comments on how to exploit this sparsity for O(nk) updates would improve readability.  

4. Significance  
  • The LKM method could be valuable when one must cluster data presented as sparse graphs (social networks, recommender systems) without explicit feature vectors. The linear‐time updates are appealing for large‐n, moderate‐k scenarios.  
  • However, the lack of theoretical convergence or optimality guarantees means practitioners may hesitate to adopt it for critical applications without further validation.  
  • The novelty is incremental rather than foundational: it adapts known local‐assignment strategies to graph‐based inputs rather than proposing a fundamentally new clustering paradigm.  

5. Suggestions for Improvement  
  • Provide even a sketch of convergence analysis: e.g., show that the objective in Eq. (11) decreases monotonically under the proposed updates, and discuss conditions under which the algorithm terminates in a finite number of steps.  
  • Compare to at least one density‐based method (DBSCAN or HDBSCAN) on “Mickey” and a standard k-means++ implementation on “Outlier” to substantiate the claim that LKM’s design is essential for these cases.  
  • Include parameter‐sensitivity plots for the choice of k (number of neighbors) on the benchmark datasets to demonstrate robustness.  
  • Tighten the notation: remove unused symbols (e.g., A in Sect. 1.1), clarify diag(A) versus diag(Y^T Y), and explicitly state when γ and λ must be chosen or tuned.  
  • Discuss the cost of graph construction (exact versus approximate k-NN) in the runtime accounting for real‐world large datasets.  

6. Minor Comments  
  • Abstract, line 5: “KSUMS and SC” is mentioned without citation and before these acronyms are defined.  
  • Line 26: the definition of “indicator matrix” A feels superfluous and is not leveraged later.  
  • Line 86: diag(A) = [a_{11}, …, a_{nn}]^T is confusing since A is not in Eq. (7) and a_{ii} entries are undefined there.  
  • Equation (9): clarify why d_{ij}^{(k)} is set to γ when xi ∉ N_k(x_j). Does this constant penalty preserve connectivity?  
  • Sect. 3.2: the update rule in Eq. (18) uses yi = e^β; should that read yi = e_β (the β-th standard basis)? A small typo.  

In summary, “Local K-means” presents a useful engineering advance by marrying k-means‐style assignments with k-NN graph inputs and delivering an O(nk) per‐iteration procedure. Yet the paper is currently held back by underdeveloped theoretical discussion, overbroad empirical claims relative to the baselines chosen, and occasional clarity/notation lapses. With deeper analysis, tighter comparisons, and polished exposition, the work could merit acceptance. As it stands, I recommend a marginal rejection.

Overall, this paper proposes a novel k-means algorithm, but I think there has not been enough discussion on its guarantees (from both a theoretical and empircal point of view) to make it convincing. The paper writing can also be greatly improved.

Originality: The mthods used in this paper are new. The paper converts the k-means objective into a different format which is compatible with nearest-neighbor graph inputs, where the main variable is the indicator matrix, and optimizes the columns in this matrix one by one (each column corresponding to the assignment vector of one point). The idea of optimizing the assignment of each point to clusters might not be new to the community, but the proposed local optization function seems to be novel.

Quality: The paper does not provide any theoretical guarantees for the proposed algorithm and mainly used empirical evaluation approaches to show its superiority. The experiments are carefully designed, but I think the authors are trying to make some claims that are too broad and thus the provided empirical results do not seem to be convincing enough to show that this approach really beats state-of-the-art. LKM is compared with a lot of baselines and that may still not cover the entire state-of-the-art clustering approaches. Some comments:
- The self-synthesized data are well designed, and serves to show that LKM performs well on some datasets whose natural clustering cannot be captured by the optimal k-means objective function. However, it seems to me that they might be suitable for other types of clustering such as density-based clustering (DBSCAN might be good for "Mickey"), or common k-means implementation packages might also be good for them (k-means++ might be good for "Outlier" depending on the sampled seeds?).  I wonder if the authors have considered these?
- After reading the experiment section I get a bit more confused about the claims that this paper is trying to make: LKM seems to be beating most baselines on the datasets in clustering quality and be significantly faster than k-means? The improvement of LKM , especially when compared with KSUMS on most of the datasets, do not seem to be very significant. Actually, the results gave me the impression that there are many datasets and each of them might be suitable for different types of clustering methods, with LKM performing relative well for all datasets.

Clarity: The writing clarity is fine, although occasionally there are some flaws such as definition/notation that has not been used again later, typos and so on, but it does not affect reading too much. It has a clear problem definition and algorithm description. That being said, I'm not very impressed with the organization of the paper. 
- The paper spent enough space on problem definition but the paragraphs did not serve to fully motivate this design. For example, previous methods like KSUMS and LKM, whose math formulation, as the authors pointed out, have very similar structure with the one used in this paper, but the authors did not provide intuition about why the differences lead to better performance.
- The description of the algorithm design is only about one page and I think there should be more intuitive explanation of why this particular design of local objective function (18) might lead to a better solution and make it robust against outliers. 

Significance: This paper could potentially propose a solution that advances state-of-the-art, but the comparsion between the new algorithm and previous approaches does not seem to convince people of its superiority. Also, I'm not sure if the improvement is significant enough for this venue.

Minor comments:
- In abstract, line 5, the authors mentioned "KSUMS and SC" without citation and before defining which algorithms these are referring to.
- line 26, "For matrix A, we call it indicator matrix", I don't really see the point of introducing the indicator matrix here, and the notation is not really used again in this paper.
- line 86, "where diag(A) = [a11,..., ann]^T",  A is not used in (7), and I'm not sure about what a11, ..., ann are referring to.
- In (9), I'm not sure about why is d_ij^{(k)} set as \gamma otherwise? Can the authors comment on this?

Limitations And Societal Impact:
The authors have adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work.

Rating:
5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: Proximal and Federated Random Reshuffling

reviews.summary: This paper studies a proximal extension of random reshuffling algorithm, namely ProxRR for finite-sum composite optimization. ProxRR requires only one proximal steps per round, which reduces the complexities incurred by costly proximal operators. This paper also studies a federated version of ProxRR, namely FedRR, and proved theoretical advantages over Local SGD.
reviews.main_review:
I would like to express my appreciation for your submission to NeurIPS 2021. The paper is overall well-written and I enjoyed reading it. My concerns are as follows.

1. The ProxRR presented in this paper applies only one proximal step per epoch. This reduces the number of calls to proximal operators, but at the same time incurs additional complexities (say, in eq (6)). It is hard to calibrate the significance of ProxRR bound (6) with [1] or Proximal SGD (7) since there are two factors mixed in --- reshuffling and reduced proximal calls. Have you considered doing ablation study to separate these two factors? For example, how does “ProxRR with proximal at every step” work? Will it eliminate the additional term in (6)?

2. This work compares  FedRR with other smooth federated optimization algorithm such as FedAvg and SCAFFOLD. How does FedRR compare with other federated composite algorithms such as [2] (theoretically or empirically)? For example, for experiments presented in Fig. 2, how does FedRR compare with FedDualAvg [2] if $\lambda_1 \neq 0$?

[1] Random Reshufﬂing: Simple Analysis with Vast Improvements, NeurIPS 2020.
[2] Federated Composite Optimization, ICML 2021.

reviews.summary: This paper proposed two shuffling based stochastic optimization methods, ProxRR and FedRR. ProxRR is the proximal version of SGD with shuffling, and they prove that ProxRR achieves better convergence rate than vanilla proximal SGD on strongly convex function, similar to its counterpart SGD with shuffling. Another advantage of this paper is that their convergence results do not need bounded gradient assumption, which was needed in the several prior shuffling SGD papers. In FedRR algorithm, each client uses SGD without replacement to optimize the local model, instead of vanilla SGD employed in local SGD. They also have analysis of FedRR for convex and nonconvex functions under both homogeneous and heterogeneous settings, and the convergence rates are faster than local SGD. The experiments also show the faster convergence of shuffling based algorithms.
reviews.main_review: As far as I know, this paper is the first to analyze the convergence of proximal SGD without replacement, and it is also the first to introduce shuffling ideas into federated optimization. The theoretical analysis is sound to me. The experiments also support their theory. The paper is well-written and both experimental and theoretical results are clearly presented. This paper introduces shuffled local updates in local SGD, which is a well-known idea to speedup SGD in the traditional optimization community. As the authors showed in the paper, this idea does improve the convergence rate of vanilla local SGD, and the experiments also support that.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact:
1. For ProxRR, I am curious about the comparison with the literature of shuffling SGD. Particularly, [1] gives a lower bound $O(n/T^2)$, so can we choose the learning rate carefully such that we can match with this lower bound? 

2.  It is a decent algorithm and it does provably improve local SGD. My minor concern is that FedRR is a simple combination of local SGD and SGD without replacement. It would be great if we can apply the shuffling idea on client level as well, e.g., for model averaging stage, server does not randomly sample a subset of clients to do the average, instead it shuffles the clients and selects subsets without replacement. It makes the algorithm look more tailored for federated learning. 

[1] Rajput, Shashank, Anant Gupta, and Dimitris Papailiopoulos. "Closing the convergence gap of SGD without replacement." International Conference on Machine Learning. PMLR, 2020.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposed two shuffling based stochastic optimization methods, ProxRR and FedRR. ProxRR is the proximal version of SGD with shuffling, and they prove that ProxRR achieves better convergence rate than vanilla proximal SGD on strongly convex function, similar to its counterpart SGD with shuffling. Another advantage of this paper is that their convergence results do not need bounded gradient assumption, which was needed in the several prior shuffling SGD papers. In FedRR algorithm, each client uses SGD without replacement to optimize the local model, instead of vanilla SGD employed in local SGD. They also have analysis of FedRR for convex and nonconvex functions under both homogeneous and heterogeneous settings, and the convergence rates are faster than local SGD. The experiments also show the faster convergence of shuffling based algorithms.

Main Review:
As far as I know, this paper is the first to analyze the convergence of proximal SGD without replacement, and it is also the first to introduce shuffling ideas into federated optimization. The theoretical analysis is sound to me. The experiments also support their theory. The paper is well-written and both experimental and theoretical results are clearly presented. This paper introduces shuffled local updates in local SGD, which is a well-known idea to speedup SGD in the traditional optimization community. As the authors showed in the paper, this idea does improve the convergence rate of vanilla local SGD, and the experiments also support that.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
1. For ProxRR, I am curious about the comparison with the literature of shuffling SGD. Particularly, [1] gives a lower bound $O(n/T^2)$, so can we choose the learning rate carefully such that we can match with this lower bound? 

2.  It is a decent algorithm and it does provably improve local SGD. My minor concern is that FedRR is a simple combination of local SGD and SGD without replacement. It would be great if we can apply the shuffling idea on client level as well, e.g., for model averaging stage, server does not randomly sample a subset of clients to do the average, instead it shuffles the clients and selects subsets without replacement. It makes the algorithm look more tailored for federated learning. 

[1] Rajput, Shashank, Anant Gupta, and Dimitris Papailiopoulos. "Closing the convergence gap of SGD without replacement." International Conference on Machine Learning. PMLR, 2020.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: In this paper, the authors propose two new algorithms for finite sum minimization: Proximal Random Reshuffling (ProxRR) and Federated Random Reshuffling (FedRR). ProxRR is used to solve composite functions, while FedRR is a special case of ProxRR as used in the context of federated optimization. They provide convergence rates under various convexity assumptions, and they also show convergence to small gradient norm (in expectation) for smooth non-convex objectives.
reviews.main_review:
In extending random reshuffling methods to composite optimization, the authors observe the importance of only applying the proximal operator once every epoch of random reshuffling, rather than at the end of each step. Thus, their algorithm only evaluates the proximal operator a single time during each pass of the data. They go on to show that their results apply to the setting of Shuffle-Once, which was also considered (alongside random reshuffling) in Mishchenko et al. (2020). The authors also provide promising experiments in the federated setting. 

That said, the results of this paper, particularly in handling the random reshuffling difficulties, are somewhat of an incremental extension when compared to Mishchenko et al. (2020). It would be recommended that the authors better elaborate in what ways their results go beyond simply combining Mishchenko et al. (2020) with the standard prox analysis.


==== Minor comments ====

The authors should refer to, and compare with, the method FedAc [1].

Line 263: The equation is overlapping the text.

The authors note there is no tuning for the ProxRR vs SGD experiments (though tuning here would be *strongly* recommended), but what about for the federated optimization experiments? Please elaborate.


[1] Honglin Yuan, and Tengyu Ma. "Federated Accelerated Stochastic Gradient Descent." In NeurIPS. 2020.

In this paper, the authors propose two new algorithms for finite sum minimization: Proximal Random Reshuffling (ProxRR) and Federated Random Reshuffling (FedRR). ProxRR is used to solve composite functions, while FedRR is a special case of ProxRR as used in the context of federated optimization. They provide convergence rates under various convexity assumptions, and they also show convergence to small gradient norm (in expectation) for smooth non-convex objectives.

Thus, their algorithm only evaluates the proximal operator a single time during each pass of the data. They go on to show that their results apply to the setting of Shuffle-Once, which was also considered (alongside random reshuffling) in Mishchenko et al. (2020). The authors also provide promising experiments in the federated setting. 

That said, the results of this paper, particularly in handling the random reshuffling difficulties, are somewhat of an incremental extension when compared to Mishchenko et al. (2020). reviews.text:
Summary:
The paper introduces two novel algorithms for structured finite-sum minimization—Proximal Random Reshuffling (ProxRR) and its federated learning specialization, FedRR. ProxRR extends the Random Reshuffling (RR) paradigm to composite problems by applying the proximal operator once per epoch rather than after every stochastic gradient step. FedRR emerges as a direct application of ProxRR to the federated setting via a consensus-based reformulation. The authors establish convergence guarantees under (i) strong convexity of individual components (Theorem 2), (ii) strong convexity of the regularizer alone (Theorem 3), and (iii) smooth nonconvex objectives (Theorems 5 and 8). They further develop extensions to decreasing step sizes (Section I), importance resampling (Section J), and provide empirical validation on logistic regression tasks and federated scenarios.

Strengths:
- ProxRR’s single-epoch proximal evaluation yields up to an n× reduction in proximal operations per epoch, which can translate into large wall‐clock gains when proximal mappings are expensive (e.g., ℓ1 regularization on dense vectors or communication‐heavy network averaging).
- Theoretical analysis elegantly combines the recent shufﬂing‐variance tools of Mishchenko et al. (2020) with proximal‐step arguments, resulting in tight linear rates to a neighborhood and O(1/ε) exact rates with decreasing steps (Sections 4 and I).
- FedRR is obtained via a clean product‐space reformulation and the consensus‐constraint proximal projection (Lemma 7). The resulting communication complexity (Corollaries 4–6) improves over standard Local SGD and even beats known lower bounds in certain finite‐sum regimes (Section 6, and comparison to Woodworth et al. 2020).
- The nonconvex treatment (Theorem 5) yields an O(1/(ε³)) rate to stationarity, matching best-known dependencies for RR with unbiased sampling.
- Experiments demonstrate ProxRR’s proximal‐call efficiency on elastic‐net logistic regression (Figure 1) and highlight FedRR’s superior convergence over Local SGD and SCAFFOLD in both homogeneous and heterogeneous label‐skew settings (Figure 2).

Weaknesses:
- The core analysis heavily relies on recent permutation‐variance bounds of Mishchenko et al. (2020) and standard prox‐SGD results; the paper sometimes reads as a modular combination rather than fundamentally new technique. It would strengthen the paper to more precisely delineate which proof elements are genuinely novel versus straightforward extensions.
- Empirical evaluation, while illustrative, omits comparisons to other federated‐optimization baselines that employ epoch‐level proximal or variance‐reduction, such as FedAc [Yuan & Ma, NeurIPS 2020], FedProx, or FedAvg with proximal regularization. Including FedAc in experiments would clarify relative advantages.
- Hyperparameter details for FedRR versus Local SGD and SCAFFOLD are brief. The authors note no tuning for ProxRR vs SGD (Section 7, Figure 1), but do not specify whether stepsizes or momentum parameters were tuned for the federated experiments. A short table of learning‐rate schedules and any grid‐search ranges would aid reproducibility.
- Line 263 in the PDF has overlapping equation text, which disrupts readability; this should be fixed.
- Though the manuscript mentions importance resampling in Section J, no experiments explore this extension. A small synthetic or real‐data illustration could substantiate the practical value of importance‐weighted RR.

Detailed Comments:

1. Novelty relative to Mishchenko et al. (2020):
   - The paper leans on the “shuffling radius” concept and bounding Lemma Theorem 1 from Mishchenko et al. The adaptation to composite objectives (via Lemma 7’s prox‐operator projection argument) is neat, but more discussion is needed on why the single‐prox per epoch trick does not follow immediately from existing prox‐SGD theory.
   - In particular, the intuition in Example 1 motivating epoch‐level proxing is concise. However, the subsequent convergence proofs (Theorems 2/3) largely parallel the vanilla‐RR arguments with an added proximal mapping at the end. It would help to highlight any subtle coupling between the proximal error and shuffling variance that required new analysis.

2. Federated Optimization Comparisons:
   - Section 6’s reformulation to the product space and analysis (Lemmas 1–2, Theorem 4) is rigorous. Yet, empirical baselines are limited to Local SGD and SCAFFOLD. Recent works such as FedAc [Yuan & Ma, 2020] or FedProx address similar epoch‐level proximal communication patterns. Please include at least FedAc in Figure 2 to quantify performance gaps.
   - The claim that FedRR “beats the best known lower bound for Local SGD” deserves elaboration: mention the exact dependence on n and M compared to Woodworth et al. (2020) and clarify the finite‐sum versus online distinction.

3. Experimental Details:
   - For the logistic‐regression experiments (Equation 12), specify the values of λ₁ and λ₂, the dimension d, and the dataset used. Report the actual proximal‐call time versus gradient‐step time to substantiate the claim of “n times faster” (Section 7).
   - In federated setups, detail how label heterogeneity was induced (sorting by label and mixing with unsorted data). Provide the average number of local steps n and global rounds T used for each method.
   - Report the standard deviation or confidence intervals in Figure 2 for FedRR, Local SGD, and SCAFFOLD to show stability across seeds.

4. Writing and Presentation:
   - Overall, the paper is well‐structured; notation is consistent, and assumptions are clearly stated in Section 3. Theorem 1’s bound on σ²_rad (Equation 5) is intuitive once one recalls the definition of population variance.
   - Please correct the overlapping text at Line 263 and recheck figure captions for clarity (reference to “xmin” in Figure 1 right plot is ambiguous—does “worst shuffle” refer to maximum over permutations?).

Recommendation:
While the proposed ProxRR and FedRR methods offer a pragmatic epoch‐level proximal approach and solid theoretical guarantees, the work reads largely as an incremental extension of existing RR analyses to the composite and federated settings. The authors should bolster both the presentation of novel analytical contributions and the experimental comparison to the broader federated‐learning literature (notably FedAc). Given these considerations, I assign a rating of 5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold. I encourage the authors to address the above points in a revision for stronger impact.
